title: Unexpected 'SLA latency penalty' surcharge on finalized invoice after hosted fallback during region failover
body:
Issue summary:
Acme Health (dedicated) found a charge labeled 'SLA latency penalty — us-east-1 — 2026-03-08 02:15–05:30' for $432.15 on the finalized invoice for billing cycle ending 2026-03-08. The customer experienced a region failover and automatic hosted fallback for ~3 hours. They assert that SLA latency penalties should not apply to dedicated customers while fallback is active, and that any surrogate routing should inherit exemption tags from the reserved contract.

Impact:
- Customer-facing: unexpected invoice line and total increase (~$432.15).
- Financial: customer requests immediate provisional credit while we investigate.
- Trust: customer concerned about billing rule correctness during fallback events.

Environment:
- Customer tier: Dedicated
- Primary region: us-east-1
- Event window: 2026-03-08 02:15–05:30 UTC
- Service components involved: smart-routing -> hosted fallba...

title: Avro incremental SIEM delta feed with per-batch Ed25519-signed manifest, tenant retention map, and compliance attachments for EU bank
body: Customer (NordBank ASA) requests a production-ready incremental SIEM export delivered in Avro container format with the following non-standard requirements: per-batch Ed25519 detached signatures, a machine-readable tenant_retention_map describing per-field retention/TTL and legal-hold flags, timezone-normalized deltas (UTC canonical plus local-bank timezone mapping for reconciliation), and a compliance evidence package (sample GLUE schema, checksum manifest, and answers to regulator questionnaire). The feed will be consumed by the bank's Splunk/ELK bridge and must be demonstrably replayable and verifiable for audit. Impact: customer cannot onboard until signed-manifest and retention semantics are validated by their security and legal teams. Request is time-sensitive due to upcoming regulator audit window.

normalized.description: Customer (NordBank ASA) requests a production-ready incremental SIEM export delivered in Avro container format with the following non-standard requirements: per-batch Ed25519 detached signatures, a machine-readable tenant_retention_map describing per-field retention/TTL and legal-hold flags, timezone-normalized deltas (UTC canonical plus local-bank timezone mapping for reconciliation), and a compliance evidence package (sample GLUE schema, checksum manifest, and answers to regulator questionnaire). The feed will be consumed by the bank's Splunk/ELK bridge and must be demonstrably replayable and verifiable for audit. Impact: customer cannot onboard until signed-manifest and retention semantics are validated by their security and legal teams. Request is time-sensitive due to upcoming regulator audit window.

comments.author: NordBank (@ nordbank.no)
comments.body: We need a signed manifest for every hourly Avro chunk; our SIEM bridge will reject unsigned batches.

comments.author: Ava Chen
comments.body: Acknowledged. Opening triage ticket and validating that ed25519 signing is supported by our signing-service. We will provide sample files and a verification script. ETA for initial sample: 48 hours.

comments.author: Ravi Patel
comments.body: Preliminary triage: platform signing-service currently issues RSA-PSS signatures via KMS-backed keys. Native ed25519 support is not live in managed signing-service. Two paths: (A) emit RSA-PSS manifest today (accepted by some customers) and include verification script; (B) implement ed25519 signing flow (requires HSM/KMS support + deployment) — estimate 2-3 weeks. Asking customer whether RSA-PSS is acceptable as interim.

comments.author: NordBank (@ nordbank.no)
comments.body: RSA-PSS is acceptable only if legal/ops can verify non-repudiation and key provenance; they prefer ed25519 but can accept RSA for POC if we supply chain evidence and timestamped manifests.

comments.author: Ava Chen
comments.body: Uploaded POC Avro file + RSA-PSS signed manifest to private S3 share and provided verification script. Requested NordBank SFTP creds to test direct delivery and confirmation that retention_map format meets their compliance team's needs.

title: Quota boundary and throttle-shepherding guidance for mixed bulk + realtime workload
body:
Issue summary: Customer BeaconMaps seeing recurring 429s during daily bulk index refresh and low-latency routing for map tile annotations. Impact: Intermittent 429s causing failed embedders and delayed annotations in production (~18% of index refresh jobs fail).

Environment: Dedicated pool (dedicated-beacon-3), prod us-west. Heavy mixed workload: scheduled bulk embeddings (multipart uploads) + real-time annotation calls from mobile clients.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Trigger nightly bulk ingest for 80k documents via multipart upload with 16 parallel workers.
2) While ingest runs, send ~200 low-latency chat/annotation requests per second pinned to the same model variant.
3) Observe rate-limit headers and return codes.

Observed behavior: Throttles (429) occur predominantly on multipart upload endpoints and the streaming annotation calls report Retry-After of 5-7s. Customer reports that r...

3) Observe rate-limit headers and return codes.

Observed behavior: Throttles (429) occur predominantly on multipart upload endpoints and the streaming annotation calls report Retry-After of 5-7s. Customer reports that r...

comments.body: 18 Support - Ava Chen: Acknowledged receipt. Escalating to infra to validate dedicated pool rate-window config. SLA first response due 2026-03-10.

02 Infra - Rafael Ortiz: Observed tenant token burn during bulk ingest; windows show sustained consumption exceeding background-burst threshold. Need more telemetry: token-window vs route-level counts. Asking customer for sample request ids.
45 Customer - BeaconMaps (S. Patel): Provided sample request ids and timeline. Reconfirmed parallelism reduction avoids 429s but is not viable long-term.
32 Support - Ava Chen: Provided interim workaround: throttle bulk upload workers to 8 and enable route-level isolation for /v1/embeddings/upload. Sent configuration steps.

15 Eng - Rafael Ortiz: Ran simulation on staging dedicated-beacon-3. Confirmed that enabling 'split_burst_pools' with a dedicated background lane for multipart uploads prevented tenant-wide 429s in test. Low-latency lane maintained latency SLOs.

40 Customer - BeaconMaps (S. Patel): Applying recommended config now; asked about billing/quota implications and whether burst credits are shared. Requesting next steps for stabilizing at production scale.

title: Provision ephemeral IAM broker role and cross-account access for runtime debugging across GCP/AWS
body:
Issue summary:
We need a short-lived, auditable broker role that lets SRE/Platform engineers assume cross-account access into customer-facing Dedicated and Private environments (AWS/GCP) for live debugging and incident response. The broker should centralize approval, rotate credentials automatically, and emit audit events to the central logging pipeline.

Background:
Recent incidents required manual cross-account key exchange and one-off service-account impersonation. That flow caused delays and missing audit context when engineers debugged GPU-backed inference stacks in customer staging and prod. We want a managed ephemeral path that enforces least privilege and automatic expiry.

Impact:
- Time-to-diagnose is increased for Sev1/Sev2 incidents affecting dedicated/private customers.
- Manual processes create orphaned access snapshots and inconsistent audit trails.

Request / Acceptanc...

title: AcmeBank (Private): audit log export has gaps during peak hours
body:
Issue summary:
AcmeBank compliance team observed missing audit log events in their Redwood Private deployment. They ingest our audit log stream into Splunk; some hours show fewer events than expected compared to their internal admin action counts.

Impact:
Low immediate app impact, but compliance reporting is unreliable; customer wants assurance and remediation plan.

Environment:
- Deployment: Redwood Private
- Region: eu-west
- Control plane: 1.14.3
- Sink: HTTP push to customer collector (then Splunk)

Notes:
Customer reports gaps correlate with peak inference traffic. No obvious 5xx errors from exporter.

comments.author: External (AcmeBank) 2025-02-17
comments.body: Confirmed: gaps occur during 09:00-10:00 CET and 16:00-17:00 CET. Our audit review script shows 12 missing RBAC changes over 2 days.

comments.body: Hotfix is safe for private tenants. Scheduling rollout with CS.

comments.body: Customer confirmed post-hotfix: no new missing events observed for 72 hours. Closing; tracking longer-term via known-issue page + postmortem for compliance narrative.

title: Jira automation: SLA reminders + triage required-field validation + known-issues index prompt
body:
Goal: Reduce time-to-triage + prevent avoidable back-and-forth by enforcing minimum triage context and keeping SLA timers visible/acted on.

This ticket implements Jira Automation rules for the Customer Support project (SUP-*) and light-touch prompts to link customers/workflows to the Known Issues hub.

Scope
1) SLA reminders
- Automated reminders when first response or next update cadence is at-risk or breached.
- Reminders should be Support-friendly (actionable, not noisy) and aligned to the new SLA policy by tier.

2) Required field validation at triage
- When a SUP ticket transitions into Triage (or In Progress), validate a small set of required fields needed for effective escalation to SRE/Eng.
- If fields are missing, block progression (or immediately bounce back to New with a clear comment) and tag the reporter/assignee with what’s missing.

3) Known-issues index prompt (auto-l...

3) Known-issues index prompt (auto-l...

comments.author: Dev Patel
comments.body: This is the #1 tooling gap coming out of the escalation playbook refresh. On-call asked for consistent context before pages; we can’t rely on training alone. Please start with dry-run comments so we can tune the required fields list.

comments.author: Dylan Brooks
comments.body:
Drafted Rule 1 + Rule 3 in Jira Automation (disabled). Need confirmation on exact field names:
- Customer Tier (single select)
- Affected Region(s) (multi)
- Model / Endpoint (text vs select)
- Impact Summary (textarea)
- Request IDs (textarea)
If any of these don’t exist consistently, I’ll align to the Jira field guidelines page and update it with exact names.

comments.author: Owen Phillips
comments.body: +1 on blocking only at In Progress. During active incidents we triage quickly and fill fields while we investigate. If we block earlier, we’ll end up fighting the workflow. Required: region, model/endpoint, impact summary, and request IDs/timestamps window.

comments.author: Rachel Kim
comments.body: For enterprise accounts, make sure reminders don’t imply we owe 30-min updates 24/7. Language should reference coverage windows (business hours vs after-hours) and say “per tier policy”.

comments.author: Dylan Brooks
comments.body: Implemented dry-run required-fields comment. Added suppression for labels security-sensitive/customer-sensitive. Next: set up scheduled JQL for first-response ‘at risk’ reminders. Need a Slack destination: #support-ops vs #support. Preference?

comments.author: Dev Patel
comments.body: Use #support-ops for reminders. #support is too noisy and will get ignored. For P0/P1 only, also @mention on-call coordinator in the comment (we can manually tune later).

comments.author: Chris Osei
comments.body: Please add a lightweight metric: count of tickets hitting triage-missing-context label and median time from New -> Triage. We want to show impact in the post-sprint metrics review.

comments.author: Dylan Brooks
comments.body: Known-issues prompt rule draft is ready but I’m holding it until we confirm keyword mapping to avoid spamming. I’ll ship SLA first-response reminders + triage dry run first, then enable known-issues prompts with limited categories (streaming + 429) as a pilot.

title: Preflight safety check requests are being billed against tenant quota and causing 429s for interactive traffic
body:
Issue summary: Customer runs a mixed workload where each interactive chat request is preceded by a lightweight 'preflight' safety/scoring check. After enabling a preflight microservice, FinSight started seeing persistent 429 throttles for low-latency interactive chat traffic. Impact: end-users get immediate 429s for short chat requests despite overall tenant usage being within expected bounds. The customer reports that each chat interaction produces two quota decrements in the console: one for preflight and one for the actual generation.

Expected behavior: preflight checks are lightweight and should either be exempt from tenant token accounting (internal preflight) or billed at a small fixed cost, not double-counting the real generation request. Observed behavior: preflight + generation both decrement tenant token buckets as full requests, leading to tenant quota exhaustion and 429s ...

comments.body: Raised by FinSight support engineer. Asked for request samples, API keys, and timestamps. Provided initial guidance to throttle preflight frequency.

comments.author: Diego Marquez (SRE) 2026-03-10
comments.body: Confirmed double-counting in rate-limiter metrics. Noted edge config change v22.4 as likely root cause. Requested edge logs for affected time range.

comments.author: FinSight- (customer) 2026-03-10
comments.body: Provided sample request pair and said disabling preflight reduces 429s immediately but is not an acceptable long-term solution.

comments.author: Diego Marquez (SRE) 2026-03-11
comments.body: Workaround validated — adding header 'X-Redwood-Internal: true' to preflight requests prevents billing. Preparing patch to restore internal-header preservation in edge routing.

title: CI Terraform run blocked: CMEK/KMS keyRing and key creation denied by org-policy during dedicated-prod onboarding
body:
Issue summary:
CI (terraform) provisioning step that creates a KMS KeyRing and CMEK key in project redwood-prod-dedicated failed with org-policy DENY on keyRing.create and serviceAccount.impersonate. This blocks onboarding of analytics-replicator-sa for Dedicated environment and prevents terraform apply from completing.

Impact:
- New Dedicated capacity onboarding is blocked (analytics-replicator service) delaying customer migration.
- CI pipeline failing on every run; engineers cannot complete infra changes requiring CMEK.
- Risk of manual key creation and out-of-band grants if not resolved quickly.

Environment:
- GCP project: redwood-prod-dedicated
- CI: github-actions self-hosted runners in prod-us-west
- Terraform version: 1.6.8; google provider: 4.79.0
- Affected regions: us-west1 (primary), us-east1 (replica)

Steps to reproduce:
1. Checkout branch infra/cmek/onboard-analytics
...

comments.author: Sagar Patel ()
comments.body: Noted the policy was part of a compliance hardening sweep; default scoping was set too broadly. Requested list of projects and principals that need exception. Suggested short-lived exception via Access Approvals for infra provisioning.

comments.body: Uploaded minimal justification bundle and proposed exception scope (projects/redwood-prod-dedicated, principals: ci-provisioner-sa, 48h window). Also documented exact resource URIs and terraform resource names for the Security review.

comments.author: Marco Lin
comments.body: Performed manual create walkthrough with Infra owner to unblock urgent customer onboarding; documented steps and audit entries in confluence. This is temporary and pending Security exception. Added follow-up task to rotate keys and remove manual grants after automation is verified.

comments.author: Sagar Patel ()
comments.body: Approved a narrow, timebound Access Approval: exception granted for 48 hours to allow provisioning; Security will validate audit logs post-provisioning and then revoke exception.

title: Dedicated (us-east) sustained throughput below committed floor for Vidorama deployment
body:
Issue summary
- Customer (Vidorama) reports sustained throughput below their Dedicated committed floor in us-east.
- Symptoms observed over multiple hours (not a transient warmup dip).

Customer impact
- Production inference throughput ~25–35% below expected; customer reports request queueing and elevated end-user latency.
- Customer states they are operating within agreed model/version + context constraints and not exceeding their committed concurrency guidance.

Environment
- Deployment: ded-vidorama-ue1-17 (Dedicated)
- Region: us-east
- Route: /v1/chat/completions (streaming enabled)
- Model pinned: llama-3.1-70b-instruct
- Workload shape (per customer): steady-state concurrency 24–40; input ~600–1,000 tokens; output ~150–300 tokens

Expected vs observed
- Expected: meet or exceed committed throughput floor (per Dedicated order form; customer quoting “committed tokens/sec”) during...

comments.body: 18 UTC — Dev Patel (Support): Acknowledged. Confirming details: deployment_id, model pin, approx concurrency + input/output tokens, and whether requests are streaming. Also requesting 3–5 request_ids during the slow windows for correlation.

33 UTC — Customer (Vidorama): Provided deployment_id=ded-vidorama-ue1-17. Streaming enabled. Concurrency ~30. Input ~800 tokens, output ~200 tokens. Slow since yesterday ~18:00 UTC. Provided request IDs: req_7d2a… / req_91bf… / req_3a0c…

02 UTC — Owen Phillips (Support Eng): Correlated request_ids in logs; no elevated 5xx. Gateway latency increases appear downstream (time to first token + overall stream duration). No 429s from customer org. Escalating to SRE/Infra with deployment_id + timeframe.

20 UTC — Aisha Bello (SRE): Checked Dedicated Throughput SLO dashboard: throughput below floor for multiple 5-min windows; burn-rate alert would have triggered at current thresholds. Saturation looks like fewer active workers than expected. Suspect pool drift / worker loss vs pure workload variance.

41 UTC — Irene Choi (Infra): Fleet view shows ded-vidorama-ue1-17 assigned 6/8 workers healthy (2 in drain/failed replacement state). Likely related to overnight host maintenance event. Working with Fleet to either replace nodes or migrate deployment to spare pool.

05 UTC — Vanessa Ortiz (Fleet): Confirmed: node drain occurred; replacement request stuck due to capacity reservation mis-match in us-east A100 pool. We have spare capacity in alternate dedicated pool; recommend migration to restore 8/8 now, then fix reservation accounting to prevent recurrence.
12 UTC — Irene Choi (Infra): Executing migration of ded-vidorama-ue1-17 to healthy pool (us-east). ETA 10–15 minutes. Expect brief connection churn; routing will drain old workers before cutover.
31 UTC — Owen Phillips (Support Eng): Migration complete. Observing throughput rising to ~8.6k–9.1k tok/s (output) and queue depth returning to baseline. Asking customer to validate from their side.
10 UTC — Customer (Vidorama): Confirmed throughput back to expected and backlog cleared. Streaming responsiveness improved. Thanks.

40 UTC — Owen Phillips (Support Eng): Closing loop. Root cause: under-provisioned worker count due to drain + failed replacement in us-east dedicated pool; mitigated by pool migration. Follow-ups filed with Infra/SRE to add worker-count drift alerting and tighten drain safeguards for Dedicated pools.

title: OgleMark: session-coordinator failover amplification causing retries, duplicate acknowledgements, and elevated 5xx rate — after-action
body:
Issue summary:
During a sustained traffic burst from OgleMark (enterprise tenant with heavy streaming usage) the session-coordinator entered a failover amplification loop. The coordinator retried session handoffs aggressively while downstream autoscaler and serving pools were already saturated. Symptoms included elevated 5xx rate (peaked at ~18% for the tenant), duplicate acknowledgement messages seen by the customer (duplicate completions for the same prompt), and streaming connections that briefly stalled then reconnected, producing overlapping responses.

Impact:
- Customer-visible: duplicate completions for chat sessions, delayed first-byte for streaming responses, occasional client disconnects.
- Business: SLA exposure for OgleMark (enterprise), customer escalation to account team, preliminary billing discrepancy for retried tokens.

Timeline (high level):
- 2026-02-18 02:13 UTC:...

Issue summary:
During a sustained traffic burst from OgleMark (enterprise tenant with heavy streaming usage) the session-coordinator entered a failover amplification loop. The coordinator retried session handoffs aggressively while downstream autoscaler and serving pools were already saturated. Symptoms included elevated 5xx rate (peaked at ~18% for the tenant), duplicate acknowledgement messages seen by the customer (duplicate completions for the same prompt), and streaming connections that briefly stalled then reconnected, producing overlapping responses.

Impact:
- Customer-visible: duplicate completions for chat sessions, delayed first-byte for streaming responses, occasional client disconnects.
- Business: SLA exposure for OgleMark (enterprise), customer escalation to account team, preliminary billing discrepancy for retried tokens.

Timeline (high level):
- 2026-02-18 02:13 UTC:...

comments.body: 23 UTC — Support (Ava Chen): Initial customer report: 'seeing repeated message chunks and sometimes duplicate completions for the same chat turn'. Escalating to SRE. SLA: P1 open.
30 UTC — SRE (Priya Nair): Investigating. Noted spike originating from OgleMark tenant. Collecting coordinator logs and traces; creating INC-20260218-01.
45 UTC — Engineering (Marcus Lee): Observed high coordinator retry loop; autoscaler lagging. Proposing throttle-and-affinity mitigation. Preparing hotfix branch for backoff logic.
05 UTC — Support (Ava Chen): Mitigation applied (throttle + affinity). Customer reports reduced duplicates; monitoring continues. Notifying accounts for immediate customer update.
10 UTC — Customer (OgleMark, Sarah Patel): Thanks for rapid response. Please confirm how token billing will be handled for these duplicates.
14 UTC — Finance (Diego Rivas): Billing review started; provisional credit placed pending log audit.
02 UTC — Engineering (Marcus Lee): Hotfix merged and canary deployed to 10% of coordinator fleet. No duplicate ACKs in canary traces. Planning full ramp today.
27 UTC — Support (Ava Chen): Full rollout complete. Customer signed up for a post-incident review call on 2026-02-21. Case resolved; action items assigned to engineering for follow-through.

title: Requests blocked after enabling region pinning (EU-only) — residency policy violations
body:
Issue summary:
Nordholm Payments enabled Hosted data residency (region pinning) to EU-only in the Console on 2025-02-21 ~09:40 PT. Immediately after enabling, production traffic began failing with deterministic residency enforcement errors.

Impact:
- ~35–60% of requests blocked for ~2 hours (spiky; correlated with model selection).
- Customer reports "sudden 409s" and inability to complete their POC transaction flows.
- Primary pain: unclear which configuration change is required (region endpoint vs allowed region set vs model availability).

Customer expectation:
- All inference and related processing stays within EU.
- If capacity is constrained, they prefer an explicit error over cross-region failover.

What we saw (customer-provided):
- HTTP status: 409
- Error code: RESIDENCY_POLICY_BLOCKED
- Message (example): "Request would be routed to us-east-1 which is not in allowed region...

normalized.description:
Issue summary:
Nordholm Payments enabled Hosted data residency (region pinning) to EU-only in the Console on 2025-02-21 ~09:40 PT. Immediately after enabling, production traffic began failing with deterministic residency enforcement errors.

Impact:
- ~35–60% of requests blocked for ~2 hours (spiky; correlated with model selection).
- Customer reports "sudden 409s" and inability to complete their POC transaction flows.
- Primary pain: unclear which configuration change is required (region endpoint vs allowed region set vs model availability).

Customer expectation:
- All inference and related processing stays within EU.
- If capacity is constrained, they prefer an explicit error over cross-region failover.

What we saw (customer-provided):
- HTTP status: 409
- Error code: RESIDENCY_POLICY_BLOCKED
- Message (example): "Request would be routed to us-east-1 which is not in allowed region...

comments.body: 06 PT - Avery Johnson (Reporter): Customer Nordholm Payments enabled EU-only pinning and now sees 409s. They’re escalating because it blocks their POC flows. Request IDs in description. Asking for immediate guidance and whether we can safely allowlist an additional EU region.

18 PT - Markus Klein (Assignee): Acknowledged. Looping in routing/gateway owners for quick validation of error contract + whether this is expected model availability behavior. Will provide customer-facing remediation steps ASAP.

26 PT - Markus Klein: Quick sanity check: are they explicitly hitting an EU endpoint (eu-west) or using default base URL? If using default, they may still be routed per our global routing layer but constrained by residency. Either way, error should include the target region + allowed list; asking customer for exact payload + which model(s) fail.

41 PT - Customer (via Avery): They are using default SDK config (no region override). Failing mostly on model redwood/llama3-70b-instruct. Embeddings failures are intermittent. They expected the platform to "just stay in EU" and pick equivalent infra there.

03 PT - Markus Klein: Confirmed from internal traces: llama3-70b-instruct currently not available in Hosted eu-west-1 for their account tier/capacity window; router tries us-east-1 as compatible region; residency enforcement blocks (as designed). Embeddings failures appear tied to the same routing attempt when eu-west pool is saturated (router tries to fail over).

12 PT - Markus Klein: Proposed immediate fixes (customer can pick): (1) Switch to an EU-available model variant (we can recommend redwood/llama3-8b-instruct for EU) OR (2) Expand allowed_regions to include eu-central-1 if available for their org (still EU) and set primary_region to eu-central-1 for better capacity OR (3) If they must use 70B now, Dedicated in EU or a temporary exception (not recommended) to allow us-east-1.
Also: update SDK/base URL to explicitly use EU region endpoint once configured to avoid confusion during troubleshooting.
25 PT - Markus Klein: Asked Platform to confirm whether eu-central-1 is enabled for their org in Hosted and whether embeddings-large is deployed in eu-west-1 for their tier.

02 PT - Sean Gallagher (SRE): Checked capacity + service map: eu-west-1 has chat pools for smaller models; 70B pool is US-only today for Hosted. Embeddings-large is in eu-west-1 but can spill under load; with pinning, spill is blocked (correct). If customer needs strict EU with higher capacity, best path is enabling eu-central-1 Hosted region for them (if available) or Dedicated EU.

18 PT - Markus Klein: Actionable customer steps drafted:
1) In Console > Residency: add allowed region eu-central-1 (EU) if available; keep cross-region fallback disabled.
2) Update client to target EU base URL (or set region config) to reduce accidental cross-region attempts.
3) Change model to EU-supported SKU for Hosted (70B not EU-hosted yet).
4) Re-test with request_id capture and confirm 2xx.
Sending to Avery for customer comms.
09 PT - Avery Johnson: Customer confirmed they can switch to an EU-supported model for the POC if we provide a recommended mapping. They still want a path to 70B EU for production (separate convo).

22 PT - Markus Klein: Provided mapping guidance: for strict EU Hosted today, use redwood/llama3-8b-instruct (chat) and redwood/embeddings-large (embeddings) in eu-west-1; avoid 70B model in Hosted until EU pool is available. For larger model needs, propose Dedicated EU. Also shared error explanation: 409 indicates deterministic enforcement and no customer content was processed outside allowed regions.

11 PT - Markus Klein: Follow-up: Customer updated model selection + pinned endpoint config to EU. Block rate dropped to near-zero; only remaining blocks were from a legacy job still requesting llama3-70b-instruct. Customer removed that path and confirmed success.

20 PT - Markus Klein: Closing as resolved. Keeping notes for rollout: we should improve Console UX to surface "model availability by region" when pinning is enabled and include a more explicit sub-reason in the message (MODEL_NOT_AVAILABLE_IN_ALLOWED_REGIONS vs generic routing-to-us-east).

title: Copy Query permalink returns 404 when retention backfill job rotates tracehook tokens
body: Issue summary: Customer reports that the 'Copy Query as Permalink' action in the Console returns a 404 when the team runs a retention backfill that triggers tracehook token rotation. Impact: external stakeholders using saved permalinks from scheduled reports get 404s and cannot access traces referenced in alerts. Environment: prod us-east during a retention backfill window. Affects scheduled report links shared externally.

normalized.description: Issue summary: Customer reports that the 'Copy Query as Permalink' action in the Console returns a 404 when the team runs a retention backfill that triggers tracehook token rotation. Impact: external stakeholders using saved permalinks from scheduled reports get 404s and cannot access traces referenced in alerts. Environment: prod us-east during a retention backfill window. Affects scheduled report links shared externally.

comments.author: Ava Chen ()
comments.body: Customer BrightLoop reported 12 permalinks returning 404; provided example permalink and timestamps. Asked for priority escalation.

comments.author: Diego Morales (SRE)
comments.body: Reproduced in staging by launching retention-backfill and copying permalink; confirmed token in permalink is v2 while active token is v3. Marked as Sev1 due to enterprise customer outage.

comments.author: Maya Patel
comments.body: Noted that rotation window is expected but permalinks should reference a stable identifier (token id) rather than raw token. Suggests changing permalink to reference a token id that can be resolved to current token server-side.

comments.author: (Tracehook) (Liam O'Connor)
comments.body: Identified code path in export-agent where we read tracehook.getCurrentToken() and inline it into permalink payload before permalink is registered. Proposed fix: switch to server-side token resolution by storing token_id instead of raw token and resolving at permalink read-time.

comments.author: Customer (BrightLoop contact
comments.body: Nora Singh): Provided list of affected permalinks and confirmed external teams could not access reports during a critical compliance window. Asks for ETA/rollback option.

title: Session pin split when routing policy updated mid-rollout causes inconsistent canary traffic and 5xxs for tenant
body: Customer reports a spike in 5xxs and inconsistent responses after we adjusted a routing policy mid-canary. Symptoms indicate session tokens were split between the old and new routing path resulting in duplicate canary assignments and model pin conflicts for pinned sessions on Dedicated capacity.

normalized.description: Customer reports a spike in 5xxs and inconsistent responses after we adjusted a routing policy mid-canary. Symptoms indicate session tokens were split between the old and new routing path resulting in duplicate canary assignments and model pin conflicts for pinned sessions on Dedicated capacity.

comments.author: Ava Chen
comments.body: Opened ticket after receiving customer alert. Customer experiencing ~11% 5xx rate on pinned routes immediately after our policy update at 14:00 UTC. Asked customer to provide request IDs and confirm whether they were using session cookies and header affinity concurrently.

comments.author: Isha Nair, Crimson Analytics
comments.body: Confirmed they send legacy 'sid' cookie and recently added 'x-tenant-affinity' header for internal A/B logic. Provided request-ids: req-33a2, req-33a7.

comments.author: Ravi Patel
comments.body: Observed dual-affinity tokens in routing logs. Hypothesis: enabling enableSticky without migrating/preserving legacy token namespace created overlapping affinity state across edge cache and session-store. Initiating edge cache invalidation for tenant.

comments.author: Lina Gomez
comments.body: Confirmed serving-runtime throws KV cache shard mismatch when model variant differs from pin. Suggested immediate rollback of routing policy change and manual flush of edge-affinity cache. Preparing a patch to preserve legacy token mapping on future policy changes.

comments.author: Ava Chen
comments.body: Rolled back routing policy at 15:22 UTC. Requested customer to retry. Customer reports error rate dropped to baseline after rollback but some sessions still saw inconsistent outputs; manual session flush recommended.

comments.author: Isha Nair
comments.body: Performed session cookie rotation and confirmed errors resolved for affected users. Asked for long-term mitigation and a formal postmortem.

comments.author: Ravi Patel
comments.body: Deployed hotfix to routing reconciler to preserve legacy token namespace when toggling sticky affinity. Added integration tests for mixed-token scenarios. Scheduling follow-up runbook update.

title: Traffic surge causes canary routing to bypass model-version pin for certain sessions
body:
Issue summary: During a large QPS spike for NovaChat, a subset of requests that had been explicitly pinned to rw-instruct-3b-v2 began receiving responses from the canary variant (rw-instruct-3b-v2-canary).

Impact: Customer reported inconsistent outputs (semantically different completions) for user sessions pinned to a stable model version. This impacted a small percentage (~3-5%) of requests over a 20 minute window, causing visible UX regressions for high-value enterprise customers.

Environment: Production (us-east). Customer uses Dedicated cluster with reserved capacity and a model-version pin configured at the route-level via the Console and API.

Observed behaviour: Pin set in route configuration appears to have been ignored for some requests; those requests were routed to canary. Canary traffic was permitted by rollout policy, but pinned sessions were expected to be excluded.

E...

comments.body: 'Pin was set at 2026-02-20T13:58Z; we saw odd completions starting ~14:02Z. Roughly 3% of traffic returned canary-style outputs.'

comments.author: (Ava)
comments.body: 'Thanks — collecting traces and routing logs. Can you also provide an example request id and the session id?'

comments.author: SRE (Diego)
comments.body: 'Found correlation with autoscaler spawn events at 14:03Z. Router-21 accepted traffic before config ack completed. Will instrument config ack path.'

comments.author: Routing lead, Priya Rao
comments.body: 'Adding high-priority patch to ensure strict application of explicit pins at decision-time even if config state is still reconciling. Preparing PR and canary.'

comments.author: Customer (NovaChat)
comments.body: 'We applied a temporary header-based workaround (x-redwood-strict-pin: true) and saw misroutes drop to near 0.'

title: Batcher synchronization glitch causing periodic p95/p99 latency surges and intermittent 504s for short streaming requests
body: Issue summary: Over the last 48 hours NimbleDocs (dedicated customer) has reported recurring p95/p99 latency spikes and a small number of 504 timeouts affecting short streaming requests. These occur in bursts approximately every 5-8 minutes and correlate with a rise in batcher flush latency on a subset of nodes. Impact: Customer reports degraded UX for their chat widget during peak hours; some requests hit 504 and are retried by clients. Frequency: spikes observed ~6–12x per hour during the incident window. Initial hypothesis: a synchronization edge-case in the continuous batcher interacting with KV cache warmup/prefetch locks under low-concurrency short-stream traffic.

normalized.description: Issue summary: Over the last 48 hours NimbleDocs (dedicated customer) has reported recurring p95/p99 latency spikes and a small number of 504 timeouts affecting short streaming requests. These occur in bursts approximately every 5-8 minutes and correlate with a rise in batcher flush latency on a subset of nodes. Impact: Customer reports degraded UX for their chat widget during peak hours; some requests hit 504 and are retried by clients. Frequency: spikes observed ~6–12x per hour during the incident window. Initial hypothesis: a synchronization edge-case in the continuous batcher interacting with KV cache warmup/prefetch locks under low-concurrency short-stream traffic.

comments.author: Arielle Kim
comments.body: Customer reported increased p99 latency in the chat widget with a few 504s. Collected sample traces and logs; escalated to SRE.

comments.author: Mateo Alvarez
comments.body: Identified pattern on ~3 nodes in NimbleDocs dedicated pool. Batcher flush latency spikes precede 504s. Tentative hypothesis: synchronous kv prefetch holds flush lock longer.

comments.author: Priya Nair
comments.body: Reproduced locally with prefetch concurrency forced to 1; p99 jumps reproduced. PR #5213 noted; rolling back prefetch concurrency as a canary in staging.

comments.author: Arielle Kim
comments.body: Informed customer about ongoing investigation and temporary mitigation plan (node drain + staged rollback). Customer acknowledged; chasing acceptance for immediate node drain.

comments.author: Mateo Alvarez
comments.body: Started rolling drain of 4 affected nodes; replaced nodes show stable batcher.flush_latency.p99 ~20ms. Monitoring for recurrence.

title: Routing escalation matrix drift affecting SLA-based routing for multi-tenant support flows
body:
Issue summary:
Support tooling routing rules have diverged from the canonical SLA escalation matrix for a subset of multi-tenant routes. Some P1 routes for Dedicated-tier customers are being escalated to L2 on the wrong schedule (30m instead of 15m), and a few self-serve routes are hitting manual-intervention macros unexpectedly.

Impact:
- Potential customer-visible violation of escalation SLA for ~4 Dedicated customers over the last 48h.
- Increased mean time to engage for urgent tickets; reported by on-call as customer-impacting but intermittent.
- Reporting shows mismatched counts between routing-engine logs and support-console SLA buckets.

Scope / affected areas:
- Routing-engine rule set for multi-tenant routes generated by sla-config-service v1.9.1
- Support console macros for escalation (templates/escalate/*)
- Reporting pipeline aggregations for SLA breach counts

title: Unexpected slot exhaustion during predictive-autoscale window causing degraded throughput
body:
Issue summary:
During the 09:00-10:00 UTC predictive-autoscale window on 2026-03-12, Nimbus Analytics reported a sustained p95/p99 throughput drop (30-60%) on their reserved pool. The pool reported full GPU slot allocation and started returning 429 throttles and occasional 503s for batched routes.

Impact:
- Customer-facing latency p95 increased from ~90ms to ~320ms and p99 from ~420ms to ~1.2s for batched text-generation endpoints.
- Several short streaming requests were failing with streaming disconnects and batch cancellations.
- Billing not affected directly but SLA risk due to degraded throughput.

Environment:
- Dedicated reserved pool (customer: Nimbus Analytics) in prod us-east-1.
- Autoscaler configured in predictive mode (lookback 6h, forecast horizon 1h).
- Pool had pinned model redwood-7b-v1 and fallback llm-open-13b-q4.

comments.author: Ava Chen
comments.body: Acknowledged. Collected initial logs and asked SRE to pull scheduler and autoscaler traces. SLA flagged and priority set to P1.

comments.author: Diego Martinez
comments.body: Initial hypothesis: slot exhaustion caused by many short-lived streams fragmenting slot capacity and predictive autoscale placing nodes with incompatible affinity. Pulling host-level GPU logs and kernel module messages.

comments.author: Nimbus SRE - Rahul Patel
comments.body: We confirmed traffic spike originates from a localized workflow with thousands of 50-200ms calls every minute; long-run sessions continued concurrently. We can throttle that workflow if needed for troubleshooting.

comments.author: Maya Singh
comments.body: Replayed customer's trace in staging. Reproduction succeeded: short-stream fanout saturates slots and causes the batcher to cancel inflight batches. Observed high KV-cache growth and increased NVLink IO wait on 3 hosts.

comments.author: Ava Chen
comments.body: Temporary mitigations applied: 1) Enabled per-tenant short-stream isolation policy (route to separate slot pool) for Nimbus; 2) Increased autoscaler affinity relaxation window. Monitoring impact.

comments.author: Diego Martinez
comments.body: After mitigation, p95 returned to 110-130ms, p99 to ~240ms. Will keep ticket open to track permanent changes and postmortem.

comments.author: Rahul Patel
comments.body: Confirmed improved performance. Asked for guidance to avoid recurrence and whether predictive mode should be used going forward.

Impact:
- Customer-facing latency p95 increased from ~90ms to ~320ms and p99 from ~420ms to ~1.2s for batched text-generation endpoints.
- Several short streaming requests were failing with streaming disconnects and batch cancellations.
- Billing not affected directly but SLA risk due to degraded throughput.

- Autoscaler configured in predictive mode (lookback 6h, forecast horizon 1h).
- Pool had pinned model redwood-7b-v1 and fallback llm-open-13b-q4.

title: Intermittent 403s for impersonated admin calls after projected service-account token rotation in private deployment
body: Issue summary: Customer reports intermittent 403 Forbidden responses when the Redwood control plane attempts to perform admin-level operations using impersonation (service-account token exchange) after projected token rotation on their private deployment. Impact: Admin console actions (user role changes, policy updates) and automated role-sync jobs fail intermittently for a subset of users, causing delayed user provisioning and blocked audit uploads. Environment: Private deployment (VPC) for Acme Health; Redwood control plane communicates with customer-managed auth-service that projects Kubernetes service account tokens into a sidecar volume. Problem first seen after IdP and K8s token lifetime changes on 2026-03-09.

normalized.description: Issue summary: Customer reports intermittent 403 Forbidden responses when the Redwood control plane attempts to perform admin-level operations using impersonation (service-account token exchange) after projected token rotation on their private deployment. Impact: Admin console actions (user role changes, policy updates) and automated role-sync jobs fail intermittently for a subset of users, causing delayed user provisioning and blocked audit uploads. Environment: Private deployment (VPC) for Acme Health; Redwood control plane communicates with customer-managed auth-service that projects Kubernetes service account tokens into a sidecar volume. Problem first seen after IdP and K8s token lifetime changes on 2026-03-09.

comments.author: Ava Thompson ()
comments.body: Customer reports ~15% of admin actions failing since they changed projected token TTL. They've paused rotation and failures rate dropped.

comments.author: Marcus Li (SRE)
comments.body: Investigating token exchange path and caching in auth-proxy. Added increased logging and a request_id trace to isolate timing. Suspect race between kube projected token refresh and token-reviewer cache TTL on the IdP side.

comments.author: Customer Success
comments.body: Noted customer cannot extend projected TTL due to security policy. Customer requests guidance for a config-based mitigation in Redwood while they revisit policy.

comments.author: Acme Health, Customer
comments.body: We see failures mostly during our nightly key rotation window. Can Redwood add a small retry/backoff or validate token freshness before exchange?

comments.author: Marcus Li (SRE)
comments.body: Proposed mitigation: 1) add short deterministic retry (3 attempts with 200ms jitter) on 403 with subcodes 'invalid_audience'/'jti_mismatch' during impersonation exchange, 2) optionally verify token 'iat' and wait up to 1s if token age < 5s before attempting exchange. Will validate with staging. Filing follow-up internal task to implement guarded retry.

comments.author: Ava Thompson ()
comments.body: Scheduled call with customer 2026-03-13 to validate staging patch. Tracking SLA milestones.

title: Quartz Commerce complaint: unwanted sales outreach triggered by false-positive PLG signal
body:
Issue Summary
- Customer (Quartz Commerce) reported receiving unexpected sales outreach referencing “Dedicated/Private options” shortly after they viewed pricing/security content.
- Customer is currently self-serve, not in an active evaluation, and explicitly stated they were “just browsing” and felt the outreach was intrusive.

Customer Report (verbatim-ish)
- "We got a sales email right after we read your security FAQ. We weren’t asking for a sales conversation and it feels like you’re tracking us. Please remove us from sales outreach."

Impact
- Reputational risk and churn risk for self-serve cohort.
- Indicates signal pipeline is generating false-positive intent signals that route directly to HubSpot tasks without sufficient confidence/guardrails.

Environment
- Signal router: prod
- CRM: HubSpot (NA pilot workflow)

What likely happened (initial hypothesis)
- An intent-only signa...

Issue Summary
- Customer (Quartz Commerce) reported receiving unexpected sales outreach referencing “Dedicated/Private options” shortly after they viewed pricing/security content.
- Customer is currently self-serve, not in an active evaluation, and explicitly stated they were “just browsing” and felt the outreach was intrusive.

Customer Report (verbatim-ish)
- "We got a sales email right after we read your security FAQ. We weren’t asking for a sales conversation and it feels like you’re tracking us. Please remove us from sales outreach."

Impact
- Reputational risk and churn risk for self-serve cohort.
- Indicates signal pipeline is generating false-positive intent signals that route directly to HubSpot tasks without sufficient confidence/guardrails.

Environment
- Signal router: prod
- CRM: HubSpot (NA pilot workflow)

What likely happened (initial hypothesis)
- An intent-only signa...

comments.body:
14 PT — Jasmine Liu (Reporter)
Customer emailed CSM alias asking to be removed from sales outreach. They are self-serve (single workspace) and not in an active eval. They said the timing felt “creepy”. Flagging as urgent to resolve + prevent repeats.

Requested: (1) stop outreach for Quartz, (2) confirm what triggered, (3) adjust rules to prevent intent-only browsing from auto-tasking.

02 PT — Grace O'Connor (Assignee)
Acknowledged. I’ll (a) pause SDR/AE touches for Quartz, (b) pull HubSpot timeline + last routed signal note, (c) coordinate with Ben Carter / David Kim on suppression + confidence thresholds.

30 PT — David Kim (HubSpot admin)
Checked HubSpot company record: task was created via “PLG Signal → NA Pilot” workflow. Timeline note shows signal tag: INTENT_SECURITY_DOCS + INTENT_PRICING within 24h. Score recorded as 0.72 (routing cutoff is 0.70). No usage threshold met.

Current workflow does not require token/spend thresholds; it routes on intent score alone.

05 PT — Ben Carter (Growth)
This matches the false-positive pattern we saw in #sales (Quartz example). Recommendation:
- Add suppression rule: “intent-only” requires either (a) explicit opt-in event (contact/demo/security review request), OR (b) intent + at least one threshold signal (sustained spend, sustained tokens, repeated 429s).
- Increase confidence cutoff for intent-only to >=0.85 during pilot.
- Add a 14-day suppression after “customer requested no sales outreach”.

10 PT — Logan Wright (Eng - Telemetry/Analytics)
Pulled signal payload (analytics table):
- Account: quartz-commerce
- Window: 2025-05-18
- Events: docs_viewed: security-questions-for-plg-upgrades (2), dedicated-upgrade-triggers (1), pricing (1)
- No correlated product usage spike; tokens/day steady and below threshold.

Agree this should not have auto-routed as a task. This is a scoring + routing guardrail gap.

22 PT — Grace O'Connor
Customer response draft below (keeping it privacy-aware and not referencing specific pages). Pending Jasmine review.

Also: David, can you mark company as “Do Not Contact” and remove from pilot workflow list until the suppression fix ships?

40 PT — David Kim
Done: set HubSpot properties:
- Do Not Contact = true
- PLG Signal Pilot = excluded
- Added note to timeline: “Customer requested no proactive sales outreach; support-managed only.”
Also removed open task and notified assigned AE.

15 PT — Marcus Lin (Security Eng)
Please ensure comms do not say “we track your browsing”. Better framing: “We sometimes use account-level engagement signals to offer help when teams scale or have security needs.” Avoid page-level details, avoid individual attribution. If they ask: confirm we do not ingest page content, and we minimize PII per data handling policy.

55 PT — Priya Natarajan (Eng sponsor)
Eng update: we’ll implement a routing change so INTENT_* signals cannot create HubSpot tasks unless:
- signal.confidence >= 0.85 AND signal has “explicit_request=true”, OR
- combined_signal = intent + threshold (spend/tokens/429s/team expansion)
We’ll also add a 30-day suppression window after a “false positive” disposition.
Targeting patch via existing suppression work (see PR for guardrails).

08 PT — Grace O'Connor
Customer replied acknowledging apology, asked: “Can you confirm you won’t contact us again unless we reach out?” I confirmed DNC flag set and we’ll only respond if they request assistance.

Also requested: can we add a lightweight internal FAQ snippet for AEs/SDRs about how to explain PLG signals if asked.

22 PT — Grace O'Connor
Confirmed suppression fix shipped + enabled for NA pilot cohort.
- Intent-only routing now requires explicit request OR combined signal.
- Added suppression: “false_positive_disposition” blocks re-notification for 30 days.
- Quartz Commerce set to DNC and excluded from pilot lists.

Closing ticket. Jasmine: please reopen if customer reports any additional outreach.

title: VPCE conditional-forwarder mismatch causing stale service IPs -> healthcheck flapping and slow canary rollbacks
body:
Issue summary:
Customer (Finlytics Corp) on a Redwood Private deployment reports intermittent serving node healthcheck flaps and delayed canary rollbacks after a routine control-plane endpoint IP change. Investigation shows control-plane A records propagated to the customer's VPC endpoint inconsistently due to a conditional-forwarder mismatch on their branch DNS resolver chain.

Impact:
- Several short-lived model serving canary rollouts experienced prolonged degraded states (canary stayed in 'degrading' for 6-12 minutes instead of expected ~30s).
- Occasional 502/504 on model inference paths during artifact fetch retries when a subset of serving nodes resolved control-plane to old IPs and attempted auth refreshes against retired endpoints.
- Customer reports user-facing latency spikes on scheduled batch jobs for ~2 hours during the propagation window.

Environment:
- Private deployme...

comments.body: 05 UTC - Alex Chen (Support): Opened ticket after customer reported canary timeouts and healthcheck flaps. Collecting resolver configs and traceroutes.
12 UTC - Priya Natarajan (SRE): Confirmed split DNS responses across customer resolvers. Suspect conditional-forwarder mismatch; asked customer for onprem-resolver config and named zone list.

01 UTC - Finlytics_NetOps (customer): Provided named zones; noted they manage branch-forwarder separately and had an automated config job that failed to push the delegated zone update to ~30% of branches.

30 UTC - Priya Natarajan (SRE): Suggested temporary TTL reduction and cache purge; infra ops agreed to temporarily add retired IPs to control-plane SANs for compatibility window. Implemented workaround at 05:46 UTC.
20 UTC - Alex Chen (Support): Monitoring shows reduced incidents after customer completed branch push and caches expired. Customer requests a postmortem and runbook update.
10 UTC - Priya Natarajan (SRE): Drafted investigations notes and recommended runbook changes. Escalating to infra-release for a permanent control-plane DNS change to reduce blast radius of rotations.

title: Elevated chat priming latency after runtime 1.7 migration (increased time-to-first-token)
body: Issue summary: After the 1.7 runtime migration (rolled out to Dedicated clusters in us-east on 2025-02-09), LinguaWorks reported elevated time-to-first-token (TTFB) for chat-completion streams. Impact: interactive chat experiences are seeing 600ms-2.2s additional latency on first token causing user-facing slow initial responses and some timeouts for low-latency SLAs. Scope: observed across multiple models on the customer's dedicated pool and correlated with runtime rollout window.

Scope: observed across multiple models on the customer's dedicated pool and correlated with runtime rollout window.

comments.author: Ava Chen
comments.body: Customer reports median TTFB ~1.1s (baseline ~250ms). Customer requests urgent mitigation because this affects demo SLAs.

comments.author: Ravi Patel
comments.body: Correlated with 1.7 rollout; opening runtime investigation with engine team. Added logs and traces.

comments.author: Marta Gomez
comments.body: Noted attention_v2 kernel added synchronous KV normalization step. Draft PR to make normalization async: https://github.com/redwood-inference/serving-runtime/pull/4590

comments.author: Ava Chen
comments.body: Customer accepted short-term workaround: pin model route to runtime-1.6-compatible variant and enable pre-warm flag. Requested ETA for permanent fix.

comments.author: Ravi Patel
comments.body: Canary patch rolled to 2 nodes with async normalization; observed p95 TTFB drop from 2.2s -> 340ms for large prompts. Monitoring in progress.

title: Create/verify PagerDuty 'Support Escalations' on-call group + schedules aligned to refreshed escalation playbook
body:
Goal
Ensure the correct PagerDuty group(s), schedules, and escalation policies exist for Support escalations and match the newly published escalation paths/coverage rules. This is required so Support can page the right on-call without bypassing #incidents or creating ad-hoc paging.

Background
During playbook refresh review, we found mismatches between the documented escalation contacts and what Support can actually page in PagerDuty (some services lack a Support-facing escalation policy; some schedules are named inconsistently; after-hours routing unclear).

Scope
- Create or update a PagerDuty escalation policy named “Support Escalations (Customer Impact)” that routes to the correct primary/secondary on-call per playbook.
- Ensure schedules exist and are populated for:
 1) SRE On-Call (customer impact)
 2) Platform On-Call (control plane/auth/SSO/audit logs)
 3) Runtime/Serving On-C...

normalized.description:
Goal
Ensure the correct PagerDuty group(s), schedules, and escalation policies exist for Support escalations and match the newly published escalation paths/coverage rules. This is required so Support can page the right on-call without bypassing #incidents or creating ad-hoc paging.

Background

Scope
- Create or update a PagerDuty escalation policy named “Support Escalations (Customer Impact)” that routes to the correct primary/secondary on-call per playbook.
- Ensure schedules exist and are populated for:
 1) SRE On-Call (customer impact)
 2) Platform On-Call (control plane/auth/SSO/audit logs)
 3) Runtime/Serving On-C...

comments.author: Dev Patel
comments.body: Creating this as a blocker for playbook adoption. Support currently pages a mix of individuals + an old PD policy that doesn’t match the new routing/coverage. Need a single stable target that matches Confluence escalation paths. Tagging Benji for implementation and Rafael for SRE sign-off.

comments.author: Benji Okafor
comments.body: Started audit in PagerDuty. Found existing escalation policy “Support-Eng Escalations” pointing to a deprecated schedule (last populated 2025-10). Also found “Platform Primary” schedule exists, “SRE Primary” exists, but no dedicated Runtime schedule. Proposed approach: create new policy “Support Escalations (Customer Impact)” that goes to SRE Primary first, then Platform Primary, then SRE Secondary. Waiting on Rafael to confirm whether Runtime should be separate or handled via SRE triage/handoff.

comments.author: Owen Phillips
comments.body: +1 to single Support-facing PD target as long as playbook makes clear: if Sev0/Sev1 and broad impact, move to #incidents and let incident commander own. For isolated customer issues, Support can page via PD but still use bridge template + link SUP ticket. Please ensure the PD target name is copy/paste-friendly for macros.

comments.author: Rafael Mendes
comments.body: Confirmed: Support should page SRE first for customer-impacting prod issues; SRE will pull in Runtime/Platform as needed. We should not force Support to decide between Platform vs Runtime at page time. Recommend policy levels: L1 SRE Primary (10–15m), L2 SRE Secondary (10–15m), L3 SRE Escalation (manager) only for P0+ customer impact. Platform Primary should be added as subscriber/notification for specific services via incident process, not as default L2 in the Support policy.

comments.author: Benji Okafor
comments.body:
Implemented new escalation policy draft:
- Policy: “Support Escalations (Customer Impact)”
- L1: SRE Primary (15m)
- L2: SRE Secondary (15m)
- L3: SRE Manager On-Call (30m)
Also created a PD shortlink in internal wiki: pd://support-escalations (internal). Next: run a test page during business hours w/ Rafael + document results. INT-332 can now reference the policy name in the Slack bridge workflow.

comments.author: Benji Okafor
comments.body: Test completed (13:20 PT): triggered a test incident via PD using the new policy; SRE Primary received page in ~30s, acknowledged in 2m, escalation did not proceed. Verified Support has permission to trigger the policy and can find it via PD search. Remaining: update internal Support directory reference + confirm no legacy policy is used in macros (coordinating with Dev Patel + INT-332).

title: Service-bot RBAC rehydration failing after Okta group rename; internal analytics access degraded
body: Issue summary: After a planned Okta group rename for the analytics team, the internal analytics-runner service-bot stopped receiving updated RBAC claims. Impact: intermittent 403s for analyst dashboards, failing nightly model observability jobs that rely on the service-bot token, and elevated support noise. Environment: prod-us-west. Steps to reproduce: 1) Trigger a new session for analytics-runner service-bot (token refresh or new login). 2) Inspect RBAC claim payload via /internal/debug/claims endpoint. 3) Observe stale group list and missing role assignments. Observed logs: identity-proxy logs show SCIM delta events processed, but rbac-cache rehydration job logs show "no eligible mapping" and many 409 duplicate-upsert errors. Hypothesis: Okta group rename produced a short SCIM backlog + a small number of duplicate membership deltas; the cache rehydration worker treats a duplicate-u...

title: Multi-NAT translation ordering causes SYN/ACK rewrite leading to gRPC KV-cache invalidation and intermittent 401s on artifact fetch
body:
Issue summary: Customer reports intermittent 401 Unauthorized when Redwood control plane requests pre-signed model shards. Failures started after customer enabled a secondary corporate NAT appliance in front of their TGW and peering route policy changes.

Impact: Model downloads for HyperBank private deployment fail ~5-10% of range download attempts, causing failed warm starts and increased latency for customer inference. No full site outage but degraded success rate and increased retries in serving nodes.

Observed failure mode: Control plane authenticated requests to S3 presigned URLs return 401; serving nodes report gRPC connection resets and kv-cache invalidation (cache miss followed by successful refetch) correlated with NAT churn.

Environment: Private deployment (VPC peering to HyperBank transit), prod us-east-1, peered TGW -> corporate NAT appliance -> internet egress. Redwood...

comments.author: Ava Chen ()
comments.body: Initial report received from HyperBank on 2026-03-12 22:50 UTC. Captured retry histogram and gave immediate guidance to reduce parallel fetch concurrency as a temporary mitigation.

comments.author: Marcus Lee (SRE)
comments.body: Confirmed SYN/ACK reorder seen in packet captures. Reproduced locally with a NAT appliance VM. Assigned INT-4410 to investigate NAT sequence handling in serving subnet routing.

comments.author: HyperBank Network (customer)
comments.body: We added a secondary NAT as part of egress hardening change window. We saw spikes exactly during our nightly prewarm job. We'll keep the old NAT active for rollback and provide detailed conntrack logs.

comments.author: Marcus Lee (SRE)
comments.body: Requested packet captures from 3 hops (serving node, TGW, corporate NAT) and a pcap from origin-facing edge. Also suggested enabling ephemeral-port-randomization on NATs and increasing conntrack_table_size temporarily.

comments.author: Ava Chen ()
comments.body: Raised customer expectation and updated SLA timer. Marked as P1 due to production impact on inference warm starts.

title: Security questionnaire: clarify whether Redwood trains on customer prompts/data (approved wording + FAQ link)
body:
Issue summary:
Multiple enterprise prospects (latest: Summit Insurance) are asking a recurring questionnaire question:
- “Do you use customer data to train models?”
- “Do you fine-tune models using our prompts/responses?”
- “Is our content used to improve your services / shared across customers?”

Impact:
This question is gating security review completion. Field teams are currently answering inconsistently (some say “never retained” which conflicts with retention/logging nuance). Need a single approved response, including deployment-mode nuances (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private) and an internal pointer to evidence/FAQ.

Customer ask (verbatim from questionnaire):
“Confirm whether the provider uses Customer Content (including prompts, inputs, outputs, and metadata) to train, fine-tune, or otherwise improve any AI/ML models. If yes, describe opt-out options and controls.”

Approved respo...

comments.author: Dev Patel
comments.body: Logging this as a recurring blocker from the field. Summit Insurance security questionnaire due early next week; AE asking for a single approved paragraph. Similar question came up in Acme Health and Aurora Financial calls as well.

comments.author: Olga Petrov
comments.body: Confirming policy posture: we do NOT use customer prompts/outputs to train/fine-tune models by default. We need to keep wording aligned with retention language (avoid “never stored”). Recommend the approved block include “by default” + “explicit opt-in + separate written agreement.”

comments.author: Marcus Lin
comments.body: +1. Also add: access to customer content (when required for support) is limited and audited. Don’t over-promise on “no employee ever sees content” because support escalation can require customer authorization and time-bound access.

comments.author: Naomi Feldman
comments.body: Please ensure this answer does not imply any future roadmap. Approved phrasing: “does not … by default” and “any use … would require explicit opt-in and a separate written agreement.” That matches our current commitments.

comments.author: Daniel Carter
comments.body: I’m aligning this with the refreshed Security FAQ. Proposed canonical link for Sales/SEs: Confluence > Sales Enablement > Security FAQ (2026) > “Data use, model training, and customer content.” Once posted, please reference the page rather than rephrasing.

comments.author: Dev Patel
comments.body: Field request: can we include a one-liner for questionnaires that ask “is customer data shared across tenants?” Suggested addition (only if asked): “Customer Content is logically isolated by tenant; Redwood does not share Customer Content across customers.” Please confirm if OK to include.

comments.author: Marcus Lin
comments.body: OK to include “not shared across customers” in this context, but keep it scoped to content (not logs/metadata), and avoid technical claims like “physically air-gapped” unless it’s Dedicated/Private-specific and confirmed in the deployment design.

title: Mesh routing flap in edge fabric causing extended TLS handshake and request timeouts for us-west customers
body:
Issue summary:
Customers in the us-west region are observing elevated request latencies and sporadic request timeouts. The symptoms trace to an edge mesh routing flap that produces prolonged TLS handshake duration and occasional connection resets when the gateway picks a cross-region egress path.

Impact:
- Multiple Clipstitch Media endpoints experienced 5-10% request failures (timeouts) during the window; p95 increased from ~120ms to ~2.1s for text generation calls.
- Affected features: streaming responses sometimes disconnect mid-stream; non-streaming generation requests hit 504s after backend timeout.

Environment:
- Prod customers in us-west region on Dedicated pools
- API endpoints: /v1/generate, /v1/stream
- Proxy layer: edge-nginx mesh with smart-routing enabled

Observed behavior:
1) Client establishes TCP but TLS handshake time increases unpredictably (handshake durations sam...

comments.author: SRE - Mateo Alvarez (2026-03-10)
comments.body: Correlated anomaly in control-plane route weights at 2026-03-10T02:14:00Z. Some nodes temporarily selected eastus peer egress for us-west traffic. Gathering node-level logs and eBPF flow captures.

comments.author: Customer - Sarah Kim (Clipstitch) (2026-03-10)
comments.body: Saw 504s around 02:14-02:20Z, replays attached. This impacts nightly thumbnail generation jobs.

comments.author: SRE - Mateo Alvarez (2026-03-11)
comments.body: Deployed hotfix to the control-plane weighting algorithm to prevent aggressive rebalancing. For immediate mitigation we forced local-egress policy for Clipstitch's dedicated pool. Monitoring metrics for 12 hours.

comments.author: Lina Park (2026-03-12)
comments.body: Customer confirmed reduced timeouts after forced local-egress, but asked for root cause and permanent fix. Escalating to infra-owner for post-mortem.

title: Transport-level queueing from client SDK connection reuse + aggressive retry policy causing perceived inference timeouts for high-throughput tenant
body:
Issue summary:
QuantisLabs reports a sudden increase in end-user perceived inference timeouts and long tail latency spikes starting 2026-03-09 21:00 UTC. Customer is on Dedicated capacity in us-east-1. They observe that a subset (~5%) of requests experience 10–30s 'hang' on the client before either timing out or finally receiving a response. Server-side metrics show healthy request completion in the same time window for most traffic, but client-side traces show queuing and repeated retry attempts.

Impact:
- High error/timeout rate for customer (approx 4% 504/timeouts) on peak traffic windows.
- Customer-facing end-to-end latency increase and SLO violations for their product.

Environment:
- Customer: QuantisLabs (dedicated plan)
- Region: prod us-east-1
- Redwood models: rw-13b-v2 (default), fallback rw-7b-instruct
- Client: QuantisLabs internal SDK v1.4.2 (NodeJS, http/2 enabled)
- ...

comments.author: | Lina Cho ()
comments.body: Logged and acknowledged with QuantisLabs. Requested SDK config and traces.

comments.author: | Devon Carter (SRE)
comments.body: Confirmed reproduce locally with load tool. Seeing client queueing prior to egress (matches customer traces). Suspect poolSize too small and retry policy too aggressive.

comments.author: SDK
comments.body: Implemented extra telemetry around http/2 stream occupancy and published a debug build for customer to test. Noted that Node http2 + keepalive semantics vary by platform--we should not assume unlimited concurrent streams per connection.

comments.author: | QuantisLabs (Customer)
comments.body: Tested debug build; reported 60% reduction in long-tail timeouts when increasing poolSize to 12 and reducing maxRetries to 2.

comments.author: | Ava Martinez ()
comments.body: Requested formal rollback/config guidance and recommended default SDK changes. Opened INT-4021 to track platform-side guardrails and telemetry improvements.

title: Intermittent request timeouts and tail-latency spikes for very large context + high max_tokens generations
body: Issue summary: Customer reports intermittent 504/timeout responses and long tail-latency for inference requests that include a very large historical context (50k+ input tokens) combined with high max_tokens for generation (20k+). Impact: Search assistant experiences ~15% request failure rate during batch windows; user-facing sessions show stalls and failed completions. Environment: Dedicated cluster serving LexiSearch workloads in us-east with reserved GPU pool (p4d-like instances) and custom batching settings (max_batch_tokens=131072, aggressive batching on). Observed behavior: Elevated 95/99th percentile latency and a subset of requests hitting 504 gateway timeouts. No consistent model crash; processes remain up but CPU and host-side memory spike. Expected behavior: Requests that fit configured hard limits should complete or stream partial output instead of timing out.

normalized.description: Issue summary: Customer reports intermittent 504/timeout responses and long tail-latency for inference requests that include a very large historical context (50k+ input tokens) combined with high max_tokens for generation (20k+). Impact: Search assistant experiences ~15% request failure rate during batch windows; user-facing sessions show stalls and failed completions. Environment: Dedicated cluster serving LexiSearch workloads in us-east with reserved GPU pool (p4d-like instances) and custom batching settings (max_batch_tokens=131072, aggressive batching on). Observed behavior: Elevated 95/99th percentile latency and a subset of requests hitting 504 gateway timeouts. No consistent model crash; processes remain up but CPU and host-side memory spike. Expected behavior: Requests that fit configured hard limits should complete or stream partial output instead of timing out.

comments.author: ] - Ava Chen
comments.body: Customer reported recurring 504s in the afternoon traffic window. They have a dedicated cluster; issue affects only very long-context requests with large generation length. Customer notices spikes happen when other jobs are concurrently running high-token batches.

comments.author: ] Triage - Miguel Torres
comments.body: Confirmed reproducer locally using a trimmed context. Observed similar tail latency and memory pressure on the host. Opening investigation; suspect batching heuristics + KV merge path causing large host-side copies.

comments.author: ] SRE - Lina Park
comments.body: Collected perf profiles for host-3 and runtime traces. Heap growth correlates to KV cache merge during request scheduling. Noted increased mutex contention in batch assembly code path.

comments.author: ] Customer - Rajesh Kumar (LexiSearch)
comments.body: Provided exact prompt files and confirmed that reducing max_tokens to 4k eliminates failures but user experience is degraded. Asking about short-term mitigation while Redwood patches runtime.

comments.author: ] - Miguel Torres
comments.body: Implemented a conservative workaround on staging: reduce max_batch_tokens per worker and enable early streaming flush; reproducer failure rate dropped from 18% -> 1.2% in stress test. Preparing hotfix for production.

title: Stealth health-sweep triggered autoscaler hysteresis and ephemeral GPU deficit in reserved pool
body:
Issue summary:
A GlyphHealth dedicated pool experienced a rapid drop in available GPU attachments for ~12 minutes during a midnight maintenance window. The incident coincided with an internal health-sweep job that opened short-lived monitoring connections across many nodes. Autoscaler entered a hysteresis loop (rapid shrink/grow backoff) and failed to reacquire slots quickly, causing a P50/P99 throughput dip for the customer's reserved routes.

Impact:
- Customer observed increased p50 latency from 120ms -> 420ms and p99 spike to 3.2s on reserved-batched routes for ~12 minutes.
- ~18% of requested attachments failed and were retried, causing additional queueing and cost overhead.
- No customer data leak; requests succeeded after retries. Customer reported degraded UX for core inference flows and opened support during incident.

Environment:
- Dedicated pool (GlyphHealth - dedicated co...

comments.body: Notified GlyphHealth with initial findings and applied manual scale. Customer reports error rate decreasing but still seeing p99 blips.

comments.author: Priya Nair
comments.body: Confirmed scheduler AttachError flood, disabled health-sweep in us-west-2. Investigating sidecar lease lifetime.

comments.author: Diego Martinez
comments.body: Health-sweep had a bug in fanout config causing simultaneous workers. Already patched staging; will push staggered schedule.

comments.author: Ava Chen
comments.body: Customer accepted temporary mitigation and requested postmortem. Escalated follow-ups to Capacity and Monitoring teams.

comments.author: Priya Nair
comments.body: Autoscaler smoothing PR merged and deployed to canary. Monitoring hooks added to alert if attach-failure > 1% for 10m.

comments.author: Liam Brooks
comments.body: Customer credit approved; emailing billing statement and incident summary to GlyphHealth contact (sarah@glyphhealth.com).

title: Halogen Energy — fanout stall causing streaming silence, duplicate responses, and invoice exposure (post-incident follow-up)
body: Issue summary: During a high-concurrency scheduled batch for Halogen Energy (enterprise, prod us-east), the request fanout path experienced a sustained stall resulting in partial streaming responses followed by duplicate completions. Impact: 11% of in-flight user sessions experienced either (a) a streaming silence lasting 6–18s before a replayed completion, or (b) duplicate completions delivered after client-side retry. Billing exposure: duplicate tokens were recorded for 0.8% of requests between 2026-03-10T09:42Z and 2026-03-10T10:26Z. User-visible effect: client-facing SDKs reported truncated transcripts and duplicated final messages; several Halogen automated workflows misinterpreted duplicates and triggered payment processing retrials. Environment: production, us-east, Dedicated pool: ded-pool-us-east-3, routing policy: capacity-first with multipath fanout to 3 backends. Time wind...

title: Conversation freeze after prolonged token generation when autoscaler rebalanced worker pools
body:
Issue summary: Customer reports that long-running streaming conversations (SSE and websocket) abruptly stop forwarding chunks to the client after ~18-25 seconds into a high-token generation. The model process continues to generate tokens (server-side progress and token counters continue), but the downstream transport layer stops emitting event/chunk frames and the client eventually times out.

Impact: Multiple production customers using persistent chat sessions experience mid-conversation silences resulting in failed user interactions and increased client-side retries. NeuroWrite Labs reported 6 user sessions impacted over a 2-hour window; business-critical workflows affected.

Environment: prod us-east, Redwood hosted inference, dynamic autoscaling enabled with burst capacity.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Start SSE chat session against /v1/chat/stream with model redwood/open-llama-7b and ...

comments.author: NeuroWrite
comments.body: 'Several user sessions hung mid-response. We saw the UI spinner continue but no tokens arrived after ~20s; retrying the request sometimes works.'

comments.author: Aisha Rahman
comments.body: 'Acknowledged — escalating to infra. Asking for logs, request ids, and region. Suggested temporary routing to dedicated pool; customer accepted.'

comments.author: Diego Morales
comments.body: 'Pinned affected customer route to dedicated pool and disabled rebalancer for that pool. Confirmed streams resumed for the pinned sessions. Captured serving-runtime and stream-proxy logs around the event.'

comments.author: Priya Chen
comments.body: 'Reproduced in staging with deterministic load generator. Confirmed session mapping mismatch when DynamicBatchRouter reassigns in-flight requests. Preparing PR for a session-handoff shim.'

comments.author: NeuroWrite
comments.body: 'Workaround restored functionality for impacted workflows; awaiting timeline for durable fix.'

title: Intermittent timeouts and doubled latency for repeated prompts: stale prefix / KV cache misses during streamed retry path
body: Issue summary: Customer (AtlasWrite, Dedicated) reports intermittent per-request latency spikes (1.8-2.5x baseline) and occasional streaming timeouts when users resubmit identical prompts or when client SDK retries a broken stream. Impact: Several customer-facing flows (autosummarize & chat history replay) experience user-facing slowdowns and ~5-10% request failures during peak traffic windows. Environment: prod/us-east, dedicated pool id d-pool-12. Observed primarily on gptx-13b-quantized and gptx-8b-finetune. Steps to reproduce: 1) Send a multi-turn conversation with a repeated prompt token prefix. 2) Force a transient stream disconnect on the client to trigger retry logic (SDK exponential backoff). 3) Observe server-side logs and latency metrics for repeated prompt ID. Expected behavior: Repeat prompt should hit warmed prefix/KV cache and return near-baseline latency with streaming...

comments.author: Ava Chen ()
comments.body: Notified AtlasWrite success team and requested sample trace ids. They provided traces: trace-az-8912 and trace-az-8919.

comments.author: Diego Martinez (SRE)
comments.body: Spun up targeted flamegraphs for d-pool-12 and confirmed elevated kv-fetch latencies aligning with customer traces.

comments.author: Serving
comments.body: Identified race in prefix eviction logic introduced in v2.8.0. Created a branch to add an explicit pre-warm on the retry hot-path and to reduce eviction aggressiveness for 'recently-accessed' prefixes.

comments.author: Customer (AtlasWrite, @atlaswrite.example)
comments.body: Provided user impact report. They requested immediate mitigation or a config knob to reduce timeouts on their dedicated pool.

comments.author: Diego Martinez (SRE)
comments.body: Deployed a temporary config to d-pool-12 to extend prefix TTL and enabled retry pre-warm. Monitoring shows p95 latency back to baseline and streaming errors falling to ~0.8% from 6%.

comments.author: Ava Chen ()
comments.body: Informed customer of temporary mitigation and ETA for full patch (see resolution). Customer accepted mitigation and requested postmortem once resolved.
