title: Private (VPC): streaming completion truncates after customer network policy update
body: Customer reports that after updating egress/NACL rules, streaming responses sometimes end early. Non-streaming calls are fine. They see FIN from their side at ~60s consistently.

normalized.description: Customer reports that after updating egress/NACL rules, streaming responses sometimes end early. Non-streaming calls are fine. They see FIN from their side at ~60s consistently.

comments.author: SE (Diego)
comments.body: Noted default idle timeouts on their NLB may be 60s; asked for LB type + settings.

comments.author: Customer
comments.body: Confirmed AWS NLB idle timeout 60s and their app does long generations w/ low token/sec.

comments.author: SE (Diego)
comments.body: Recommended increasing NLB idle timeout and/or enabling periodic SSE keep-alive 'data:' comment frames from the gateway.
Customer increased timeout to 300s; truncations stopped. Marking resolved.

title: Hosted API: client-side context deadline exceeded on streaming chat (us-west)
body:
Issue summary:
- Customer reports frequent failures in their Go client: `context deadline exceeded` while using streaming chat.

Impact:
- 1-3% of requests fail; retried requests succeed.
- Most failures occur after ~45-55s of streaming.

Primary symptom: Request timeouts
Deployment offering: Hosted API

Notes:
- Customer is behind a corporate proxy; may be closing idle connections.
- However, we also see periodic pauses in token emission on our side during the failures.

Requested:
- Confirm whether gateway idle-timeout or upstream flush buffering changed in recent deploy.

title: Intermittent hanging streaming responses tied to Typescript SDK default timeout
body:
Issue summary: Customer using redwood-sdk-typescript reports streaming requests sometimes appear to hang. In their logs, the HTTP request remains open but no additional chunks arrive; they eventually abort.

Impact: User experience degraded; they time out at 30s and treat as failure.

Environment: Hosted API, OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions, Node 18, fetch-based client.

Notes: They are on a mobile carrier NAT sometimes; they suspect network flakiness.

Environment: Hosted API, OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions, Node 18, fetch-based client.

Notes: They are on a mobile carrier NAT sometimes; they suspect network flakiness.

comments.author: Naomi Feldman ()
comments.body: Customer description: 'stream just stalls' (no error). They abort after 30s. Any known issue?

comments.author: Jasmine Liu (SDK)
comments.body: Our TS SDK sets a default request timeout but doesn't reset it on streamed bytes. For SSE, this makes long responses look like a hang.

comments.author: Ethan Park (Platform)
comments.body: Heartbeat should keep the connection active, but client-side overall timeout still applies.

comments.author: Jasmine Liu (SDK)
comments.body: Shipped SDK patch: disable overall timeout for stream by default; expose `streamTimeoutMs`.

comments.author: Naomi Feldman ()
comments.body: Customer confirmed fix after upgrading to redwood-sdk-typescript 0.8.4.

title: Python SDK: streaming chat breaks when tool calls appear (JSON decode error)
body: Customer uses tool calling with streaming chat. When the model emits a tool_call, the Python SDK streaming iterator raises JSONDecodeError while parsing an SSE chunk. Customer provided the raw chunk which includes an escaped newline inside the tool arguments.

normalized.description: Customer uses tool calling with streaming chat. When the model emits a tool_call, the Python SDK streaming iterator raises JSONDecodeError while parsing an SSE chunk. Customer provided the raw chunk which includes an escaped newline inside the tool arguments.

comments.author: (Rohan)
comments.body: Repro in our sample app using model rwd-llama-3.1-70b and tool schema with long string args.

comments.author: (Priya)
comments.body: Root cause: our SSE parsing assumes each event's data payload is a single JSON object per line. Tool call payload can contain newlines and gets split across data lines.

comments.author: (Priya)
comments.body: Fix: accumulate data lines per SSE event before JSON decode; add tests for tool_call streaming frames. Released python SDK 1.8.7.

comments.author: (Rohan)
comments.body: Customer confirms streaming tool calls now work.

title: TypeScript SDK: EventSource-based streaming chat freezes in long-running sessions
body: Customer reports TS SDK streaming chat intermittently freezes after ~20-30 minutes of continuous use (many conversations). They use the browser build with EventSource transport. No error; onmessage stops firing. Workaround is full page refresh.

normalized.description: Customer reports TS SDK streaming chat intermittently freezes after ~20-30 minutes of continuous use (many conversations). They use the browser build with EventSource transport. No error; onmessage stops firing. Workaround is full page refresh.

comments.author: (Vanessa)
comments.body: Collected browser version, memory profile, and a minimal repro that creates many EventSource instances.

comments.author: (Dylan)
comments.body: Found we were leaking EventSource handles when consumers stop iterating early; should close() when iterator is GC'd/aborted.

comments.author: (Dylan)
comments.body: Implemented explicit .return() handler on async iterator to close EventSource; added docs for always breaking the loop with try/finally. Released 2.4.2.

comments.author: (Vanessa)
comments.body: Customer confirmed freeze no longer occurs with 2.4.2.

title: Clarify residency guarantees and cross-region fallback behavior for region-pinned requests and expected latency impact
body:
Issue summary:
Customer wants a clear statement of the platform's data residency guarantees when a customer pins traffic to a specific region (eu-west) and how cross-region fallback behaves when that region has capacity constraints. They are specifically worried about: whether any customer PII or unredacted request payloads may leave the pinned region during fallback, what controls exist to prevent cross-region routing, and the expected latency implications if fallback to another region occurs.

Requested outcome:
- A precise explanation (for compliance) of whether request bodies, logs, or cache artifacts can be transferred to another region during fallback.
- Documentation pointers and console settings that enforce strict in-region residency (if available).
- Typical latency delta numbers for eu-west -> us-east fallback for streaming and non-streaming generation at 256/1024 token len...

- Typical latency delta numbers for eu-west -> us-east fallback for streaming and non-streaming generation at 256/1024 token len...

comments.author: Ayesha Kapoor ()
comments.body: Created ticket after customer escalation. Attached trace exports and requested an urgent compliance statement and perf numbers.

comments.author: Routing
comments.body: Confirming routing behavior. Smart Routing respects pin but will fallback when eu-west lacks capacity or when a configured policy allows cross-region fallback (default is allowed for enterprise tenants). Collecting exact code paths and policy names.

comments.author: Alice Chen ()
comments.body: Noted that audit sinks differ between Hosted and Private. For Hosted, logs are retained per global retention policy; for Private, we can configure local-only audit sink. Asked customer which deployment model they plan to use.

comments.author: Nimbus Finance, Marco Rossi
comments.body: Nimbus confirmed they aim to use Hosted (enterprise) for initial rollout. They requested an explicit statement that ROI data will not be stored outside EU persistently and asked for an opt-out of cross-region fallback until legal sign-off.

comments.author: Routing
comments.body: Follow-up: Provided temporary mitigation options: 1) disable automatic fallback in project settings (console flag: allow_cross_region_fallback=false) which will cause increased 429s instead of routing out-of-region; 2) configure stricter capacity thresholds and dedicated region-reserved capacity (Dedicated) to avoid fallback; 3) for Hosted, we can add a request to review tenant policy to enforce EU-only for this customer as a special case (requires SOC/Legal approval). Asked Product/Sales to escalate if needed.

comments.author: Ayesha Kapoor ()
comments.body: Sent lab latency numbers and the temporary mitigation steps to customer. Asked Nimbus to confirm whether they want the console flag changed or to pursue a tenant-level policy exception.

title: Enterprise customer requests shadow evaluation metrics during upcoming model upgrade rollout (quality gates + dashboard/API)
body: key: SUP-28451; project: customer-support; issue_type: Support Request; summary: Enterprise customer requests shadow evaluation metrics during upcoming model upgrade rollout (quality gates + dashboard/API); status: In Progress; priority: P1; severity: Sev2; created_at: 2026-01-29; updated_at: 2026-02-02; reporter: Monica Patel; assignee: Paula Mendes; customer_company: Northstar Customer Support AI; customer_tier: enterprise; components: console; rollouts; smart-routing; telemetry; evals-scoring; labels: shadow-traffic; quality-metrics; rollout-safety; enterprise-request; timeline-pressure; beta-access; environment: prod (us-east, eu-west) - Dedicated

comments.body:
14 PT — Monica Patel (Support/Platform Enablement)
Acknowledged request from Northstar. Confirmed we can discuss limited beta access for shadow traffic + quality deltas. Asked for:
- target rollout date
- routes to include
- required metrics (must-have vs nice-to-have)
- data retention constraints (raw prompts allowed?)

Set expectation: likely advisory quality signals at launch; automatic rollback based on quality is not available yet.

Next: loop in Paula Mendes (Rollouts) + Jonas Weber (Evals) + Logan Wright (Telemetry) for feasibility and a proposed plan by EOW.

02 PT — Customer (Northstar, Platform Eng lead)
Timeline: change review on 2026-02-06, rollout starts 2026-02-10 if approved.
Must-haves:
- shadow 2% production traffic
- format adherence + refusal drift + instruction-following rubric score
- dashboard with confidence bands (or at minimum: a daily summary with statistical significance)
- export 100–200 paired examples for audit
Constraints:
- retain prompts <= 7 days
- prefer hashed user identifiers; avoid storing full conversation histories if possible

They are OK with advisory gates but need a "recommended action" summary for reviewers (e.g., green/yellow/red).

41 PT — Paula Mendes (Rollout policies)
We can support shadow sampling at the route level with deterministic bucketing (consistent hashing) and strict fail-open. For beta enablement we should:
- cap sampling at 2% (customer request fits)
- cap targets to 1–2 shadow variants
- ensure shadow request timeouts are < 150ms budget for routing path (async send)

Need SRE sign-off on guardrails and a kill switch plan. Also need to confirm that the Console view for quality deltas is available behind the feature flag for this tenant.

10 PT — Jonas Weber (Evals/Scoring)
Metric feasibility:
- Format adherence: we can score tool-call envelope compliance + JSON parse success; strict schema validation requires customer-provided schema (not in v1). Proposed: v1 includes JSON parseable + required key presence.
- Refusal rate: can score via rubric template on shadow outputs; need to calibrate on their domain prompts (risk of false positives).
- Instruction-following: available as model-graded rubric; will pin rubric version for comparability.

Caveat: model-graded scoring adds compute cost; for 2% sampling we should be OK if we constrain max tokens captured/scored and use asyn...

55 PT — Allison Grant (Observability/Alerting)
Reminder on cardinality: no per-user or per-conversation IDs as metric labels. We can store hashed IDs in event payload for sampled examples only (separate store), but dashboards must aggregate by:
- tenant
- route
- model/control vs shadow
- region
- rubric_version

Proposed dashboards: quality delta overview (24h + rolling 6h) + scoring lag. Alerts: scoring lag and missing-data should page only if it impacts multiple tenants; for single tenant create ticket notifications.

22 PT — Sean Gallagher (SRE)
Guardrails required before enabling on an enterprise tenant:
- circuit breaker if shadow target error rate > 10% for 5 min OR scoring queue lag > 30 min
- hard budget: user-path p99 latency delta <= 1% (shadowing must be strictly async/non-blocking)
- kill switch in control plane and documented rollback steps

If these are met, SRE OK with limited beta enablement for Northstar. Please ensure audit log entries are enabled for experiment creation/edits.

18 PT — Monica Patel
Sent proposed plan to customer:
Option A (recommended): Enable limited beta shadow experiment for chat route at 2% sampling, 1 candidate variant, 7-day retention, redaction on by default. Provide Console dashboard access for quality deltas + export of paired examples.
Option B (fallback): If feature flag approval slips, run eval-harness prompt-set scoring using their provided prompt set and compare against candidate model offline; use this as change-review evidence.

Asked customer to confirm:
- whether streaming is enabled on their chat route (affects partial capture)
- whether they can provide a representative prompt...

47 PT — Customer (Northstar)
Confirmed:
- Chat route is streaming-enabled for ~60% of traffic.
- They can provide a 300–500 prompt set for calibration by 2026-02-03.
- They want shadowing to cover streaming requests, but are OK if we only capture the final assembled output (not token-by-token).

They asked for written confirmation that user-path latency will not be impacted and that they can disable the experiment immediately if they see issues.

title: Clarify strict EU residency behavior and routing when dedicated capacity scales out of region
body:
Issue summary:
Customer (HealthMetrics EU) is concerned that requests from their prod:eu-west tenant briefly routed to us-central during a dedicated pool autoscale event. They want confirmation that no customer data leaves the EU and an explanation of configuration options to prevent cross-region failover.

Impact:
- Intermittent latency spikes (200–600 ms extra) observed during two autoscale events on 2026-03-09 and 2026-03-10.
- Customer compliance team flagged any cross-border inference as unacceptable unless approved (GDPR/data residency policy).

Environment:
- Tenant: healthmetrics-eu-prod
- Deployment: Redwood Private (VPC) with Dedicated pool (reserved capacity) in eu-west region
- Routing policy currently appears to be the default: soft_preference (prefer region but allow fallback)
- Models: redwood-7b-instruct (primary), fallback redwood-13b-quant

Steps to reproduce / obser...

Steps to reproduce / obser...

comments.author: HealthMetrics EU
comments.body: We see the audit log entries that indicate some inference calls were served from us-central. Can you confirm whether customer payloads ever left the EU, and how to disable that fallback?

comments.author: Maya Chen
comments.body: Thanks for filing — assigning to routing/SRE to investigate. Can you attach the .csv export of audit entries?

comments.author: Ravi Patel ()
comments.body:
I pulled the customer's audit lines and correlated them with autoscale events. Initial hypothesis: tenant routing policy is 'soft_preference' which permits cross-region fallback when local pool capacity is low.
Action: validating exact config flag in tenant record and checking whether control-plane telemetry included any payload metadata leaving the VPC.

comments.author: Leah Foster
comments.body: We confirmed serving nodes in us-central handled ~0.6% of traffic during the window. No plaintext payloads traversed Redwood control-plane — for Private deployments the inference connections are proxied but payloads remain inside customer VPC unless explicit external fallback is used. We still need to validate implications for strict residency compliance.

comments.author: Omar Ruiz
comments.body: Per encryption and KMS config, inference payloads are TLS-encrypted in transit and at-rest in any region. However, the customer requirement is stronger: they require zero serving outside EU. We'll provide an option to disable fallback routing and a recommended capacity plan.

comments.author: Ravi Patel ()
comments.body: Proposed next steps posted to customer: 1) Enable strict_residency flag in tenant routing to prevent cross-region failover (will return 503 when eu-west cannot serve), 2) Add dedicated reserved burst capacity for eu-west to avoid rejects, 3) Run a short canary with our infra team to validate behavior. Requested approval from customer to schedule the config change and reserve capacity estimate.

title: Atlas Payments requests audit evidence of regional failover actions (who/what/why/when) for enterprise compliance
body:
Customer (Atlas Payments, enterprise) request:

Atlas Payments Security/Compliance team is asking for audit evidence of all routing/failover actions during regional impairment, specifically:
- When Redwood initiated a regional failover / traffic shift (or recommended one)
- Whether it was automated vs. manually approved
- The evidence/signals used (reachability/5xx burn/tail latency/capacity)
- Who approved/executed the action (user/service identity)
- When rollback occurred and why
- Retention period and how Atlas Payments can retrieve evidence for internal audits (SOC2 controls / change management)

Context:
- They noticed transient routing behavior during the planned controlled failover window (mid-Jan) and want an audit trail to attach to their internal change record.
- They also want a forward-looking answer: how Redwood will provide audit logs for future regional events.

Impact...

Atlas Payments Security/Compliance team is asking for audit evidence of all routing/failover actions during regional impairment, specifically:
- When Redwood initiated a regional failover / traffic shift (or recommended one)

- Who approved/executed the action (user/service identity)
- When rollback occurred and why

Context:
- They noticed transient routing behavior during the planned controlled failover window (mid-Jan) and want an audit trail to attach to their internal change record.
- They also want a forward-looking answer: how Redwood will provide audit logs for future regional events.

Impact...

comments.body: 12 PT — Owen Phillips (Support Incident Liaison): Created per Atlas Payments email follow-up. They want evidence of failover actions + retention statement. Assigning to Dev Patel for customer-facing response; looping in Allison Grant / Miles Thornton for correctness.

03 PT — Dev Patel (Support Escalation Owner): Acknowledged. I’ll draft customer response with:
1) what we log today (recommendations/executions/rollbacks)
2) example record (redacted)
3) retention + access path
4) limitations (DNS/provider routing outside of policy engine). Requesting eng confirmation on final event schema fields and retention defaults.

28 PT — Miles Thornton (Failover architecture lead): Audit event stream is in place for controller decisions. It emits structured events for:
- failover.recommended
- failover.executed
- failover.rollback
Includes policy_id, from_region, to_region, reason_category, evidence summary, and actor (service + optional human approver). See PR-19824. We can provide a redacted sample from the 2026-01-15 controlled test.
Caveat: DNS-level behavior is separately observable; not all provider-side traffic steering is captured as an audit event unless it results in a policy change we own.

10 PT — Allison Grant (Observability lead): Retention: audit events are stored in the central event store (default 30 days) and copied into our compliance archive for enterprise accounts (90 days) upon request/contract addendum. For Atlas we can offer 90 days as a best-effort now; longer retention requires account-level enablement and cost approval. Access: today = Support-provided export; Console export is planned but not yet generally available.

02 PT — Dev Patel: Confirming with Security guidance re: what customer can receive (PII/tenant identifiers). Also need to ensure sample includes only their tenant + any shared infrastructure identifiers are generalized.

40 PT — Dev Patel: Drafted response for Atlas Payments. Key points:
- We generate a failover audit trail whenever automation recommends/executes/rolls back a routing policy change.
- Events include timestamp, environment, affected route/model group, source/target regions, decision type, evidence summary, and actor/approver.
- We can provide a redacted export for the 2026-01-15 controlled test and any subsequent routing changes affecting their traffic.
- Retention: 30 days standard; enterprise retention options available (90+ days) depending on enablement.
- Limitations: provider-side DNS/anycast propagation time is not itself an “audit act...

15 PT — Victor Alvarez (Network reachability lead): Add one sentence that we can correlate to network reachability probes (cross-region) which are part of evidence signals; but DNS TTL/propagation is best-effort and varies by resolver. We don’t guarantee per-client steering timelines beyond our SLO measurement methodology.

06 PT — Dev Patel: Sent customer response via email with:
- 1-page explanation
- sample event (redacted)
- retention statement
- offer to set up recurring export during Q1 while Console self-serve export is being finalized.
Asked customer to confirm whether 90-day retention meets their audit requirement; offered longer retention discussion via CSM.

34 PT — Dev Patel: Customer replied: 90-day retention is acceptable for now; requested one more export covering 2026-01-15 00:00–06:00 PT with all rollback events included. Coordinating with Allison to generate export.

20 PT — Allison Grant: Generated export from event store for the requested window. Includes recommendation + execution + rollback entries and actor/approver fields. Redacted internal hostnames; kept policy IDs and regions. Provided to Dev for delivery.
05 PT — Dev Patel: Delivered export + short readme to Atlas Payments. They confirmed receipt and that it satisfies their internal change record. Marking Resolved.

title: Request: one-click rollback for quantization profile experiments (restore prior known-good profile quickly)
body:
Customer request (Northbridge Analytics, Dedicated PoC)

Summary
- Customer is actively experimenting with quantization profiles (int8/fp8 variants) to reduce cost.
- They want a fast, low-risk rollback mechanism when a profile causes quality regressions or latency spikes.
- Current rollback requires manual edits + redeploy/pin changes, and they’re concerned about blast radius and time-to-mitigate.

What happened / context
- During a profiling session on llama-3.1-70b-instruct, the customer switched from a baseline profile to a more aggressive quant profile.
- They observed:
 - Quality regression (more refusal / factual drift) in a small prompt set.
 - Increased tail latency (p99) during peak concurrency (likely cache/batching interaction + kernel choice).
- They were able to mitigate by manually reverting to the previous profile, but it was slow and required Redwood assistance to con...

- They were able to mitigate by manually reverting to the previous profile, but it was slow and required Redwood assistance to con...

comments.body: 14 PT — Colin O'Donnell (Reporter): Logged per Northbridge request from PoC check-in. They want a single rollback action for quant profile experiments; current manual revert + confirmation is too slow. Asking for auditability + visible active version.

02 PT — Support (via Colin): Sent interim guidance: keep baseline profile pinned; use narrow canary scope; document profile IDs; if urgent, revert profile immediately and we’ll help confirm active version using internal debug headers/logs.

26 PT — Arjun Desai (Applied ML): Agree this is a recurring pain point. From quant side, rollback must revert the *exact* profile variant and kernel settings; partial apply can create mixed behavior across workers. Strongly recommend tying rollback to versioned Optimization Config + runtime idempotency work.

40 PT — Paula Mendes (PM): Confirmed this maps to Safe Apply + Rollback initiative (Optimization Config versioning). We will include quant profile rollback with quality gates (eval harness) in limited GA, with audit log + RBAC. No commitment to fully automated rollback for external customers in v1.

05 PT — Northbridge (Customer): Thanks. Main need is: (1) obvious “active quant profile/version” in Console, (2) rollback in <5 minutes with clear scope, (3) audit log coverage for change + rollback. They’re fine with human-confirmed rollback modal.

18 PT — Colin O'Donnell: Marking resolved as “tracked on roadmap.” Shared interim workaround doc + offered session with Applied ML to set regression thresholds. Will re-open if customer hits repeated regressions before rollout.

comments.body: 14 PT — Markus Klein (Reporter): Creating ticket per beta onboarding sync. Northpeak wants burst enabled before their Wed indexing run. Simran/Miguel are aligned. Need Product/Fleet/SRE approvals per checklist in PM-541.

02 PT — Lauren Bishop (Product): Approved for beta enablement under default silver tier. Keep max_burst_ratio <= 1.75 for first week; we can revisit after we have denial/latency data. Please ensure metering is enabled and customer sees usage in Console.

18 PT — Vanessa Ortiz (Fleet): us-east/H100 burst pool has headroom this week. OK to proceed with +8 GPU tenant cap. If we see pool saturation >80% during their window, we may need to lower caps temporarily (runbook).

41 PT — Sean Gallagher (SRE): Approved with conditions: (1) SLO gate enabled for non-burst tenant p95 delta; (2) confirm global circuit breaker alerts are wired; (3) notify #eng-sre when enablement is live. If we trip the gate, we will disable burst first and follow up with customer.
09 PT — Benji Okafor (Assignee): Acknowledged. I’ll confirm allowlist + apply tenant config. Targeting completion by EOD today; will include audit log event IDs in completion note.

26 PT — Benji Okafor (Assignee): Pre-checks: us-east/H100 allowlist is ON; global kill-switch is OFF. Verified admission-controller healthy in us-east. Proceeding with tenant config update for tnt_8f3c1b2e.

41 PT — Benji Okafor (Assignee): Tenant config updated: burst_enabled=true, max_burst_ratio=1.75, per_tenant_burst_gpu_cap=8, cooldown=900s, priority=silver. Audit events emitted: BURST_ENABLED, BURST_LIMITS_UPDATED (see audit log search with tenant_id). Propagation confirmed in admission controller within ~2m.

05 PT — Benji Okafor (Assignee): Validation: routed a canary load test (internal) against dedicated endpoint; observed burst admission working and denial reasons surface correctly when cap hit (BURST_TENANT_CAP_REACHED). Metering export shows burst_gpu_seconds > 0 for test run (marked beta).
22 PT — Miguel Santos (CS): Confirmed with customer. They can see burst usage panel in Console (minor lag ~2–3m). We’ll monitor during Wed indexing window and report back.
30 PT — Benji Okafor (Assignee): Posted enablement details to #customer-success and #eng-sre (tenant_id, caps, rollback: disable burst_enabled + reduce cap to 0).
11 PT — Sean Gallagher (SRE): Observed during first burst window: pool saturation peaked 62%, non-burst p95 delta within gate. No action required.
20 PT — Benji Okafor (Assignee): Marking resolved. If Northpeak requests higher cap/ratio, please open a follow-up INT ticket and tag Fleet + SRE for re-approval.

title: EmberSearch (Dedicated): Dedicated pool upgrades need phased rollout with objective gates + fast rollback
body:
Account: EmberSearch (Dedicated)
Primary contacts: (via TAM) Head of Platform Eng + oncall rotation

Issue summary
Customer is requesting a safer, deterministic process for Dedicated pool upgrades (model version/runtime build/routing policy changes) using phased/staged rollouts with measurable gates and quick rollback. They explicitly do not want “big bang” pool flips.

Why this is urgent / escalation context
- EmberSearch runs embeddings + reranking traffic with strict latency SLOs and spiky batch workloads.
- They experienced customer-visible impact during the last 2 Dedicated pool upgrades.
- They have internal change management requirements (platform sign-off) and want a documented, auditable process.

Impact history (customer-reported)
1) 2024-12-11 (us-east): pool upgrade caused p99 latency to jump ~2.5x for ~25–35 minutes after cutover; intermittent 5xx; rollback required.
2) 2...

Primary contacts: (via TAM) Head of Platform Eng + oncall rotation

Issue summary

- EmberSearch runs embeddings + reranking traffic with strict latency SLOs and spiky batch workloads.

Impact history (customer-reported)
1) 2024-12-11 (us-east): pool upgrade caused p99 latency to jump ~2.5x for ~25–35 minutes after cutover; intermittent 5xx; rollback required.
2) 2...

comments.body: 12 PT - Liam O'Rourke (TAM): Logging per EmberSearch escalation. They want staged upgrades with gating + rollback. Last upgrade was painful and they’re requesting commitments before next quarterly upgrade window.

03 PT - Rafael Mendes (SRE): Please pull concrete timestamps from the 2025-01-17 event. If it’s the same pattern as the early beta 429 issue, likely capacity partitioning/routing interaction. We should avoid manual “split pool in half” procedures without clear headroom rules.

41 PT - Lauren Bishop (PM, Dedicated): This matches what we’re building (Dedicated staged rollouts for reserved pools). I can join customer call and position MVP: manual gates first, then automated gate evaluation + Console evidence panel.

18 PT - Allison Grant (Observability): If we commit to gating, we need stage-tagged metrics with strict cardinality controls. We can provide a limited set of standard queries for latency/error/429/saturation and show them in Console as evidence.

57 PT - Mina Haddad (Routing): Multi-region pools complicate partial upgrades. We should be explicit: stage applies per-region partition but promotion should be coordinated. Also define behavior if a region is impaired mid-stage (hold vs abort).

22 PT - Sean Gallagher (SRE): Gate proposals should be tied to their SLO and error budget burn. We should also include a ‘no promote during incident/high burn’ rule. Missing metrics should fail-closed with manual override, otherwise we’ll ship a footgun.

06 PT - Liam O'Rourke (TAM): Customer provided desired thresholds (informal): 5xx < 0.2%, p99 < 900ms, 429 < 0.5% during canary; wants 30m windows and ability to extend. They’re okay with Redwood-defined defaults as long as they can see what we’re evaluating and why we blocked a promote.

35 PT - Lauren Bishop (PM, Dedicated): Completed technical deep dive with customer. We aligned on default stages (1/10/50/100) and documented HOLD + ABORT semantics. Action: we’ll enroll EmberSearch in limited beta once feature flag is available; interim workaround is operator-run canary using a separate shadow pool only for batch indexing traffic (not ideal).

28 PT - Connor O'Brien (Release Eng): Beta enablement will be behind feature flag; we can target a controlled enablement for 1–2 accounts after migrations. Please file an internal enablement request when ready (INT ticket).

04 PT - Lauren Bishop (PM, Dedicated): Creating Product escalation notes: customer needs gate evidence visible in Console (not just internal dashboards) and audit trail export. Also requesting replica-based stages for small pools to avoid 429s from partition starvation.

26 PT - Liam O'Rourke (TAM): Filed internal request to enable staged rollouts beta once available (linked INT-1933). Customer agrees to be design partner; wants a written runbook and a ‘go/no-go’ checklist for each promotion.

50 PT - Rafael Mendes (SRE): Added EmberSearch to candidate list for beta with SRE monitoring plan. Recommendation: start with manual gates + strict hold/abort; do not allow auto-promote for first rollout. Ensure headroom validation (min replicas) to avoid a repeat of the 429 issue.

title: EU data residency & region pinning: observed cross-region fallback causing increased latency
body:
Issue summary:
Customer (NimbleHealth) reports that despite configuring region pinning to eu-west for their production account, a subset of inference requests during peak load appear to be routed to us-west as fallback. This is resulting in a measurable latency increase (200-400ms) and raises a data-residency/legal concern for EU PII-sensitive workloads.

Impact:
- Elevated end-to-end latency for critical API calls (observed +200–400ms p95).
- Customer worried about EU data residency guarantees for audit/compliance.
- Affects 3 production services using rw-open-7b-v2 for on-call triage and gpt-compat-3.5 for summarization.

Environment:
- Redwood hosted API (prod) with account region set to eu-west.
- Customer is enterprise tier with dedicated routing preference configured in console under Account -> Routing.

Steps to reproduce (as provided by customer):
1) Set region pin to eu-west ...

comments.author: Aisha Patel ()
comments.body: Acknowledged. Assigned to routing/SRE for triage. Asking customer for sample request IDs and account id. (Provided examples above.)

comments.author: Evan Morales ()
comments.body: Thanks for the logs, NimbleHealth. We're looking into SmartRouting fallback behaviour. Can you confirm if you have "allow_cross_region_fallback" enabled on the account-level routing policy? Also sending instructions to pull more detailed traces (x-trace).

comments.author: NimbleHealth (Customer)
comments.body: Confirmed they did not intentionally enable cross-region fallback. They shared additional x-trace headers and their account id: nh-prod-87.

comments.author: SRE (Liam O'Connor)
comments.body: Initial hypothesis: under capacity pressure fast-failover path used a global pool to maintain latency SLOs which can cross regions if "strict_pin" is not set. Observed pod logs show occasional borrow from us-west pool at 03:17 UTC. Referencing routing-config/pull/428 which touched fallback thresholds.

comments.author: Evan Morales ()
comments.body: Notified customer that we found borrow events and are coordinating a config change to honor strict account pin. Sharing temporary mitigation steps.

comments.author: NimbleHealth (Customer)
comments.body: Confirming they applied the temporary settings and saw no cross-region serves in a 2-hour window; asking for permanent guarantee and audit note for legal.

title: Guidance for tenant-level optimizer bailout and snapshot validation after latency and cost spike
body:
Issue summary: BrightPost observed a simultaneous latency and per-token cost spike after a tenant-scoped optimizer profile rollout (dynamic-batching + aggressive KV prefetch + q4_0 quant profile). Impact: multiple customer-facing routes experienced 95th percentile latency increase from 220ms to ~1.2s and token cost estimates doubled for a 2 hour window. Environment: Dedicated pool (dp-03), prod-us-west, tenant: brightpost-prod.

Observed at 2026-03-10 17:06 UTC via Console alerts and customer complaint. Customer requested immediate guidance for a safe bailout (hot-exit) and verification that the tenant snapshot used for rollback is consistent (no partial KV/cache mismatches) before traffic switchover.

Desired outcome: Provide a low-risk, tenant-scoped bailout procedure that reverts the optimizer changes for the tenant but preserves session continuity where possible, and a determinist...

title: Tenant-level optimizer harmonization support and safe rollback after mixed-profile anomalies
body:
Issue summary: CloudScribe (dedicated tenant) reported divergent text outputs and intermittent p95 latency spikes after a tenant-scoped optimizer rollout that combined a new quantization profile with an aggressive batching policy and KV prefetch tweaks. The customer attempted to revert the change via the console but observed mixed-model behaviour and some requests still routed to a quantized variant, causing both quality drift and tail latency.

Impact: Several production routes for CloudScribe show increased 95th- and 99th-percentile latency (from baseline ~120ms p95 to 480-650ms p95) and two customer-facing services report a small but measurable quality regression on generation prompts (token-level repeats and truncated completions). Traffic is ~300 rps for the tenant in us-west. No data exfiltration suspected; logs show increased cache evictions and recompile events.

Environment: ...

comments.body: 12 UTC - Support (Asha): Acknowledged. Opening investigation and cc'ing Runtime and SRE. SLAs noted.
45 UTC - Runtime Eng (Diego): Can you attach a sample request ID where output truncated? Also need the tenant's optimizer snapshot version. Added INT-4021 to check router cache state.

02 UTC - Customer (CloudScribe - Priya Rao): Provided request IDs: rq_00123, rq_00176, rq_00201 and optimizer snapshot 2026-03-09-03:12:07. We attempted Console rollback at 2026-03-11 02:10 UTC but saw intermittent results.

20 UTC - SRE (Marta): Observed elevated cache evictions on nodes gpu-wst-07 and gpu-wst-09 coincident with deploy. Recompile spike on runtime pods pointed out. Investigating memory pressure and kernel selection fallback.

05 UTC - Support (Asha): Proposed safe plan: 1) Quarantine tenant to dedicated routing gate, 2) Disable optimizer flags for the tenant from control plane, 3) Perform staged traffic split to fallback model, 4) Restore tenant snapshot if needed. Customer approved maintenance window 2026-03-13 02:00-03:00 UTC.

10 UTC - Runtime Eng (Diego): Started staged traffic split to 80/20 -> 50/50. Observed p95 drop to ~210ms at 50/50 in us-west. Some requests still showing quantized variant; router cache invalidation routine triggered.
33 UTC - Support (Asha): Following up to request post-rollback diff reports and to schedule a post-mortem if quality regression persists.

Impact: Several production routes for CloudScribe show increased 95th- and 99th-percentile latency (from baseline ~120ms p95 to 480-650ms p95) and two customer-facing services report a small but measurable quality regression on generation prompts (token-level repeats and truncated completions). Traffic is ~300 rps for the tenant in us-west. No data exfiltration suspected; logs show increased cache evictions and recompile events.

title: Clarify how traffic-steering and partial pinning interact with data residency and observed latency variance
body: Customer asks for an explanation of why a subset of inference requests routed through cross-region traffic-steering still appear in audit logs as handled in the non-pinned region, and why those requests have 20-150ms additional latency compared to strictly pinned requests. They say they enabled partial pinning (pinning only for inference[:generate]) and a best-effort traffic-steering policy for embeddings. They want to ensure no customer PII leaves EU while keeping cost/latency tradeoffs.

title: Cache hit-rate dropped after model update on Dedicated route; sudden cost increase traced to version_mismatch misses during rollout
body:
Issue summary:
Aurora Support (Dedicated) reported a sudden increase in inference cost and a noticeable latency shift immediately following a model update on their primary chat route. Initial hypothesis was a cache regression or telemetry issue; investigation showed a real cache efficiency drop driven by version_mismatch misses during a staged rollout that temporarily mixed model versions within the same dedicated pool.

Impact:
- Customer-reported cost increase: ~18–25% higher token charges per request (driven by higher prefill compute due to low reuse)
- Elevated p95 latency on prompts with repeated system + policy prefixes (customer expects high prefix reuse)
- Duration: ~6–10 hours (from rollout start until rollback / rollout policy adjusted)

Customer context:
- Deployment: Dedicated (single region)
- Workload: support-agent chat with long, stable system prompt and repeated polic...

Customer context:
- Deployment: Dedicated (single region)
- Workload: support-agent chat with long, stable system prompt and repeated polic...

comments.body: 02 UTC (Owen Phillips, Support): Customer reports ‘cost jumped ~20% after yesterday’s model update’ and ‘responses feel slower’ on Dedicated. Asked for timeframe, route name, and whether they pinned model version or used latest.
18 UTC (Customer - Aurora): Change was applied around 16:00 UTC. They use the ‘latest’ channel for rw-llama3.1-70b-instruct. Noted stable system prompt and expected caching to keep costs low.

35 UTC (Allison Grant, Observability): Checked cache hit-rate dashboards for rt_8f2c…: hit-rate down sharply; miss reasons show large version_mismatch starting ~16:10 UTC. No sign of metrics pipeline drop. Shared screenshot + query in #eng-sre.

52 UTC (Ethan Park, Platform Tagging): Confirmed model_version_id tag is present and consistent; join between gateway route id and runtime metrics looks correct. Likely real mixed-version rollout behavior.

10 UTC (Omar El-Sayed, Runtime): Sanity check: version_mismatch misses are expected if requests bounce across versions; cache keys include model_version_id. Suggested mitigation: avoid mixing versions for a single high-reuse route, or use sticky routing during rollout.

22 UTC (Connor O'Brien, Releases): Reviewed dedicated rollout settings: rollout set to 50/50 for several hours (customer-initiated). Proposed: revert to 0% / old version immediately, then re-run rollout with pinning or separate canary route.

40 UTC (Owen Phillips, Support -> Customer): Explained findings: cache reuse is version-scoped; during rollout with mixed versions, hit-rate dropped leading to higher prefill and cost. Proposed immediate rollback to restore baseline and then plan a safer rollout strategy.
05 UTC (Customer - Aurora): Approved rollback / revert. They want guidance on how to canary without impacting overall cost.
20 UTC (Connor O'Brien, Releases): Rollout set to 0% (old version only). Monitoring hit-rate recovery.

05 UTC (Allison Grant, Observability): Hit-rate trending back up; version_mismatch miss reason returned to near-zero. p95 latency improving. Marked incident mitigated; continue monitoring for 2 hours.
12 UTC (Miguel Santos, CS): Customer confirms cost/latency back to baseline overnight. Requested a written best-practices note for future rollouts (pinning vs canary route split).

30 UTC (Owen Phillips, Support): Added guidance to customer: (1) pin model version for primary prod route; (2) create a separate canary route with new version; (3) optionally enable sticky routing to version during rollout if they must canary on same route; (4) schedule rollouts during low traffic to reduce cache warmup cost.

15 UTC (Allison Grant, Observability): Closed loop: no additional anomalies. Recommendation: add a lightweight console note / doc callout that mixed-version rollouts reduce cache reuse and can increase cost for prefix-heavy workloads.

title: Granular optimizer snapshot + staged fallback preflight for tenant experiencing tail-latency spikes
body:
Issue summary:
Customer (AuroraAnalytics, dedicated) reports intermittent 95p+ tail-latency spikes (up to 3.5s) after a recent optimizer profile rollout that adjusted batching thresholds and enabled a new KV-prefetch heuristic on two quantized models. Impact: production traffic across several routes for analytic summarization is affected, user-visible latency spikes and increased error budgets.

Environment:
- Dedicated pool in prod-us-west (us-west-2)
- Models: redwood-mini-1-quant8 (primary), gptj-6b-int8 (fallback)
- Autoscaling policy: reserved 4 GPUs, burst to 6
- Recent changes: optimizer profile v2026-03-11 applied to tenant; KV-prefetch enabled + aggressive batching target 48 ms.

Steps to reproduce / Observed pattern:
1) Normal traffic steady state shows p50 ~120ms, p95 ~420ms.
2) After profile rollout, during moderate burst patterns (10–30 RPS per route) p95 jumps to 2.0–3.5...

2) After profile rollout, during moderate burst patterns (10–30 RPS per route) p95 jumps to 2.0–3.5...

comments.author: Aisha Patel
comments.body: Opened ticket after AuroraAnalytics CS reported elevated tail latency following optimizer update. Customer provided replay bundle and console links. Marking P1 due to production impact.

comments.author: Marco Alvarez
comments.body: Assigned. Initial hypothesis: KV-prefetch may be increasing tail work for some request shapes -> causing occasional long-running batches. Will run preflight snapshot comparison and a staged throttle test in staging using the customer's replay.

comments.author: Lina Chen
comments.body: Ran replay in staging with opt-profile:v2026-03-11. Observed identical tail spikes when KV-prefetch concurrency was set to 4. When we reduced prefetch concurrency to 1, p95 returned to baseline. Need a safe per-tenant rollback approach; customer can't afford a full revert during US business hours.

comments.author: (Aisha)
comments.body: Customer asks for a plan that allows a short safety hold (no config drift for 24h), per-tenant partial throttle, and the ability to snapshot/restore routing quickly if behavior persists. Also wants confirmation that no cold-cache penalty will occur if we change the prefetch concurrency.

comments.author: Marco Alvarez
comments.body: Prepared a stepwise preflight and rollback playbook (see investigation_notes). Proposed staged rollout: 1) snapshot current tenant optimizer and routing -> attach a time-limited hold flag; 2) apply conservative prefetch settings (concurrency=1, disable aggressive batching); 3) monitor 15-min windows and increase back gradually; 4) if regressions persist, restore snapshot to last-known-good profile. Asked SRE to review the automated snapshot/restore script.

title: Assistance requested: staged holdpoint and gradual recovery for aggressive optimizer profile on Brightpost tenant
body:
Issue summary:
Customer Brightpost Health enabled an aggressive optimizer bundle (async-batching + kv-prefetch + quant8) in their dedicated pool across a staged rollout yesterday 2026-03-11. After ~6 hours of ramp, they started reporting increased 95/99 p99 latency and a higher rate of transient 5xxs for streaming chat routes. They request an operator-assisted, low-risk plan to place a holdpoint on the rollout and bring latency back to baseline with minimal customer disruption.

Impact:
- Production customer-facing latency: 95th increased from 120ms to 480ms; p99s spike to 1.8s.
- Increased 5xxs on streaming session start (~0.7% -> 3.4%).
- Affects Brightpost dedicated pool only (tenant isolation present).
- Business: degraded user experience for brightpost.com live assistant.

Environment:
- Dedicated pool: brightpost-dedicated-01 (GPU A100s).
- Region: us-east-1.
- Active optimizer ...

title: High P95 tail latency after enabling new quantization profile + KV prefetch — request assisted safe-toggle checklist
body:
Issue summary:
Customer deployed two optimizer changes in prod earlier today: a new quantization profile (profile id qp-2026-03-13-v3) and enabled kv_prefetch=true at the serving-group level. Within ~30 minutes they observed a sharp increase in P95 token latency from ~120ms to ~620ms and intermittent request timeouts for long streaming requests. The issue impacts multiple routes and is causing user-facing latency spikes for LexiHealth's triage assistant flows.

Impact:
- Increased P95 token latency across affected model variants.
- Streaming sessions hitting websocket disconnects after 10–20s in ~5% of requests.
- Business impact: slowdown of patient triage flows; customer marked as Priority-1.

Environment:
- Redwood Dedicated pool: rwd-dedicated-lexi-01 (prod us-east)
- Serving groups: sg-lexi-triage-a, sg-lexi-triage-b
- Model variants: redwood-open-7b-quant-8bit (pinned), gptx-13b...

- Serving groups: sg-lexi-triage-a, sg-lexi-triage-b
- Model variants: redwood-open-7b-quant-8bit (pinned), gptx-13b...

comments.author: Aisha Patel ()
comments.body: Acknowledged ticket and requested customer logs and exact rollout IDs. Asked for traffic percentage routed to each serving group.

comments.author: LexiHealth (Customer)
comments.body: Provided Console audit links and sample perf histograms. Traffic routing: 60% sg-lexi-triage-a, 40% sg-lexi-triage-b. Confirmed both groups received same config changes.

comments.author: Marcus Reed ()
comments.body: Performed initial triage. Noted CPU/io pressure on nodes. Asked SRE to check node-local SSD health and IO queue depth on rwd-dedicated-lexi-01 cluster.

comments.author: Jamie Liu
comments.body: Node disks healthy; io depth shows sustained queue depth spikes concurrent with high kv_prefetch reads. No underlying storage errors. Recommended disabling kv_prefetch on one serving group as controlled test.

comments.author: Marcus Reed ()
comments.body: Executed controlled disable on sg-lexi-triage-b (reverted kv_prefetch to false for that group) via redwoodctl patch. Observed immediate P95 improvement on sg-lexi-triage-b within ~3 minutes. sg-lexi-triage-a still elevated.

comments.author: Marcus Reed ()
comments.body: Proposed full rollback plan for customer: (A) disable kv_prefetch globally, (B) revert quant profile to previous stable id qp-2026-03-09-v2, (C) perform rolling restart of serving group pods with canary routing. Asked customer for approval window.

comments.author: LexiHealth (Customer)
comments.body: Approved immediate actions. Requested step-by-step commands and monitoring checks; asked for a 1-hour maintenance window starting 16:30 UTC.

comments.author: Marcus Reed ()
comments.body: Documented exact rollback commands in ticket and scheduled the maintenance window. Noted linked PR for quant profile revert: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/1892.

title: Helios Health: audit log export format + retention wording confirmation for public case study write-up
body:
Issue summary:
Helios Health is reviewing a draft public case study and requested exact technical details for audit log export formats and retention so their Legal/Compliance team can approve any security-related statements.

Customer questions to answer (for public write-up accuracy):
1) Export format: Are audit logs available as JSON/CSV? Is it JSONL? Is the schema documented and stable?
2) Export transport: Can Redwood export to customer-controlled storage (e.g., S3) and/or SIEM? Is it pull (API) or push (delivery job)?
3) Timestamping: Are timestamps UTC? Is there an ingestion timestamp vs event timestamp?
4) Fields included: Do events include actor identity, action, resource, source IP, request ID/trace ID?
5) Retention: What is the default retention in Redwood Console? Can it be configured (e.g., 90/180/365 days)?
6) Private deployment nuance: In Helios’s VPC deployment, do audi...

6) Private deployment nuance: In Helios’s VPC deployment, do audi...

comments.body: 18 PT — Miguel Santos (Reporter): Helios compliance team asked for specifics on audit log export + retention before they’ll approve any public statement. They flagged prior phrasing as too vague (“comprehensive audit logs” / “long-term retention”). Can SE/Security confirm: export format (JSON/CSV), delivery method, default retention, and max configurable retention for Private/VPC?

02 PT — Markus Klein (Assignee): Acknowledged. Pulling exact behavior for Private/VPC audit logging and export options. Looping in Simran for implementation details + Security for approved public claim language. Target: respond to Helios by EOD tomorrow with a short, quotable paragraph + optional technical appendix for their internal review.

37 PT — Simran Kaur: Confirmed current audit log export behavior for Private/VPC:
- Audit events are recorded in the Private control plane and visible in the Redwood Console (customer tenant).
- Export is available via API (customer can pull) and via scheduled delivery to customer-controlled object storage when configured (commonly S3-compatible). For Helios’s deployment, delivery stays within the customer’s VPC networking path (no public endpoint required) assuming VPC endpoint / private routing is configured on their side.
- Export format: newline-delimited JSON (JSONL). We can also generate CSV via the API for ad-hoc pulls, but the cano...

10 PT — Rachel Kim: Adding CS guardrail: Helios is sensitive about any statement implying immutable/WORM storage or guaranteed retention periods due to healthcare requirements. Suggest we phrase as “configurable retention” + “export to customer-controlled storage for longer retention,” and avoid stating a specific maximum unless Security signs off.

46 PT — Markus Klein: Security & Compliance review outcome (via #security-help): OK to state “JSON audit logs” and “configurable retention,” and OK to state “export/stream to customer-controlled storage” as long as we don’t claim ‘tamper-proof/immutable’ and don’t imply certifications.
Proposed public case study language (approved internally):
“Helios runs Redwood Private in a VPC and uses Redwood audit logs to track administrative and API events. Audit events can be exported in JSON format to customer-controlled storage for compliance and retention needs.”
Optional technical appendix for Helios internal reviewers:
- Export format: JSONL (...

05 PT — Miguel Santos: Forwarded to Helios with two sections: (1) public-safe wording for case study draft and (2) technical appendix for their compliance team. Helios asked one follow-up: can we say “streamed to SIEM” or only “exported to storage”?

22 PT — Simran Kaur: We should avoid “streamed to SIEM” as a default claim. We can say “integrates with existing logging pipelines” only if Helios confirms their specific setup (e.g., log forwarder from their storage/SIEM). Suggested phrasing: “exported to customer-controlled storage and can be forwarded into their security tooling.” This keeps it accurate without implying a native SIEM connector.

31 PT — Markus Klein: Agree. Updated recommended wording:
“...exported in JSON format to customer-controlled storage, where it can be forwarded into existing security and compliance tooling.”
This avoids promising a first-party SIEM integration. Marking ticket resolved unless Helios requests additional details or a schema doc link.

2) Export transport: Can Redwood export to customer-controlled storage (e.g., S3) and/or SIEM? Is it pull (API) or push (delivery job)?

title: Private deployment: DNS resolution failures + TLS handshake errors after upgrade to Redwood Private 2.14.0
body:
Issue Summary
- Customer reports that immediately after upgrading Redwood Private (control plane + private gateway bundle) from 2.13.2 -> 2.14.0, their application cannot reach Redwood endpoints in the private deployment.
- Symptoms alternate between:
 1) DNS resolution failures for internal Redwood hostnames (NXDOMAIN / SERVFAIL)
 2) TLS handshake failures when DNS does resolve (x509 / unknown authority / handshake alert)

Customer Impact
- ~65–80% of requests to /v1/chat.completions and /v1/embeddings fail from the customer’s application subnets.
- Partial recovery observed when customer routes traffic through a different subnet / DNS forwarder.
- Dedicated customer-facing product endpoints impacted; customer reports degraded chatbot feature for internal employees.

Environment
- Deployment mode: Redwood Private (customer-managed VPC, AWS us-east)
- Upgrade window: 2025-11-18 16:40Z...

comments.body: 18Z — Dev Patel (Support): Opened Sev1. Customer reports post-upgrade failures: DNS SERVFAIL + TLS unknown authority. Requested dig + curl -v from two subnets and gateway endpoint details.
46Z — Rafael Mendes (SRE): Incident created (INC-2025-11-18-priv-conn-helios). Suggest classifying as Private Connectivity. Pulling in Victor for networking + Private gateway context.

12Z — Victor Alvarez (Private connectivity SME): Looking at patterns: DNS resolver intermittently SERVFAIL. Can customer share resolver IPs and conditional forwarder rules? Also confirm if gateway cert chain changed in 2.14.0 release notes.
44Z — Customer (Helios Bank): Shared outputs. Subnet A uses resolver 10.12.4.20 (SERVFAIL). Subnet B uses 10.12.4.21 (stable). TLS failures only on older AMI hosts (Amazon Linux 2 2022.03 build).

07Z — Fiona McCarthy (Security Ops): TLS error consistent with missing intermediate CA in client trust store. Recommend installing provided chain or updating ca-certificates package. Not a security incident; tracking under reliability/private connectivity is fine.

15Z — Victor Alvarez: Mitigation confirmed: customer pinned conditional forwarding to healthy resolver and updated trust store on impacted hosts. Error rate dropped to <1%. Monitoring for an hour before resolving.
33Z — Dev Patel: Marking incident resolved; keeping ticket open pending customer confirmation + follow-up checklist updates.

10Z — Dev Patel: Customer confirms stable for 5 business days, all subnets/hosts passing TLS. Closing ticket. Added note to taxonomy validation: subtype DNS/TLS under Private Deployment Connectivity; ownership primarily Private Deployments with Infra consulted.

title: Dedicated customer questions on GPU pool drain notices during regional failover (expected impact, timing, and guarantees)
body:
Customer (Atlas Payments, Dedicated) asked for clarification on Redwood drain/failover procedures after receiving a heads-up from their CSM about upcoming reliability testing and automation.

Customer questions (verbatim-ish):
1) What does “drain the Dedicated pool” mean operationally? Are requests terminated or redirected?
2) What impact should we expect (dropped requests, increased latency, streaming disconnects)?
3) How much notice will we get for a drain (planned test vs unplanned regional impairment)?
4) If our primary region is drained, do you automatically route to the secondary region? Do we need to change anything client-side?
5) Is there a way to ensure our pinned model version remains consistent across regions during a failover?
6) What are the rollback conditions if the secondary region becomes saturated?
7) Can Redwood provide evidence/audit trail of when drain/failover a...

comments.body:
18 PT — Owen Phillips (Support) — Intake/triage
Customer reached out via email thread with their CSM. No incident currently; classify as Question (P2) due to dedicated SLA sensitivity + upcoming test windows. Assigning to Dev Patel; adding SRE point of contact for correctness on drain semantics.

02 PT — Dev Patel (Support, assignee) — First response to customer (sent)
Shared high-level explanation:
- “Drain” means we stop placing new work onto specific GPU nodes/pools and allow in-flight requests to complete, then gradually shift traffic to available capacity (often in another region) according to routing policy.
- Typical customer-visible impact target: minimal. During real impairments or sharp capacity shifts, you may see a short window of elevated tail latency and some retries; we aim to avoid sustained elevated error rates.
Asked the customer:
- confirm their primary/secondary regions and whether they use streaming for chat wo...

36 PT — Lauren Bishop (Dedicated Product) — Product guidance
Reinforced expectations for Dedicated:
- Dedicated pool drain is sequenced and gated by headroom checks in the destination region.
- Planned tests should have proactive notice (target: 3–5 business days) for enterprise/Dedicated accounts; unplanned drains during impairment follow incident comms cadence.
- Model pinning: we maintain pinned versions across supported regions for Dedicated where configured; if a pinned artifact isn’t present in the secondary region, routing should not silently switch model versions without explicit policy (confirm per account).

12 PT — Rafael Mendes (SRE) — Technical accuracy on drain/failover semantics
Provided internal notes to support response:
- Drain workflow: stop new placements -> reduce weights / shift routing -> monitor error/latency -> evacuate remaining capacity if needed.
- In-flight streaming: may disconnect if region redirect occurs mid-stream; guidance is to reconnect with idempotency key / request_id. (We recently shipped improvements; still recommend robust client retry/backoff.)
- Rollback: automated anti-flap timers and safe-rate limits; if secondary region saturates, we shed load with retry-after instead of cascading failures.
- Auditability: ...

05 PT — Dev Patel (Support) — Consolidated customer answer (sent)
Sent a structured answer addressing each question:
1) Drain meaning: new traffic is gradually shifted; in-flight requests are allowed to complete where possible; we avoid hard-killing connections except in severe impairment.
2) Expected impact: for planned drains, target is no downtime; possible brief tail-latency increase. For unplanned failover, there can be transient 5xx/timeouts during detection + convergence; client retries are recommended.
3) Notice: planned test windows—proactive notice for Dedicated accounts + status page note if applicable; unplanned impairment—stat...

47 PT — Customer (Atlas Payments) — Follow-up
Customer confirmed:
- Primary us-east, secondary eu-west is acceptable from a compliance standpoint.
- They do use streaming for chat on ~35% of requests.
Requested:
- confirmation that their Dedicated secondary has enough warm capacity for peak,
- confirmation of how far in advance model artifacts are staged in secondary region.

30 PT — Vanessa Ortiz (Fleet/Capacity) — Capacity check summary
Provided account-specific guidance to Support (no raw numbers shared externally):
- eu-west pool has sufficient headroom for Atlas baseline + a controlled failover test; during peak hours, capacity margin is tighter.
- Recommend planned drain/failover test during off-peak and confirm autoscaling guardrails are enabled.
- If customer expects a sharp spike during test window, we should coordinate a temporary burst reservation.

10 PT — Dev Patel (Support) — Final customer update (sent)
Confirmed:
- We can schedule any planned drain/failover test during their off-peak window.
- We will ensure pinned model artifacts are staged in eu-west prior to any planned activity.
- For streaming workloads, we reiterated reconnect guidance and offered a short pre-flight test in staging.
Customer acknowledged and indicated no further questions.

22 PT — Dev Patel (Support) — Resolution
Marking Resolved. No product bug identified; provided procedural explanation + planned test coordination.
Follow-ups:
- If customer requests audit evidence formally, route to SUP-25444 pattern (exported audit log + retention statement).
- Add Atlas Payments to the proactive notification list for planned regional failover tests (coordinate with CSM + comms owner).

title: Private deployment: tenant-alias in 'aud' claim allows scheduled worker token but blocks interactive console access
body:
Issue summary:
During routine verification after a configuration change to the customer's IdP, Citrine reported that scheduled background workers using service tokens could access model execution routes, but their web console users received 403 when performing the same actions. This started after an IdP update that began including an alternate tenant alias in the JWT 'aud' claim.

Impact:
- Scheduled ingestion and reconciliation workers (CRON jobs) continued to execute successfully (no failures observed in job runner logs).
- Interactive web console operations for tenant admins and engineers (model pinning, rollout checks) are intermittently denied 403.
- Customer reports failed UI operations during business hours causing operational disruption.
- Potential risk: audit logs show different principal identity for worker vs console actions which complicates compliance auditing.

Environm...

comments.body: Opened ticket after receiving initial report from Citrine. Customer attached token dumps and request traces.

comments.author: Citrine (Alice M., SRE)
comments.body: We rolled IdP change yesterday; can confirm only interactive logins show failures. Scheduled jobs unaffected. Need help triaging - blocking day-to-day ops.

comments.author: Lucas Nguyen ()
comments.body: Reproduced 403 in staging by replaying multi-valued 'aud' token. Adding a sandbox test to confirm sanitizer branch behavior. Linked a PR to prototype normalization: https://github.com/redwood-inference/platform/pull/4832

comments.author: Marina Ortiz ()
comments.body: Please ensure any normalization preserves the set of audiences for auditing. We cannot silently drop alias values without recording them.

comments.author: Lucas Nguyen ()
comments.body: Engineering plan: add normalization in ingress that flags multi-valued aud, normalizes canonical id for RBAC match, and emits original aud array to audit logs. Target staging deploy 2026-03-15.

comments.author: Citrine (Alice M.)
comments.body: We can stop emission of alias on our SSO side if you need extra time. Please keep us posted on ETA for the fix.

title: Northstar Financial (Dedicated) requests SLO + error budget reporting and definitions (availability/latency/streaming)
body:
Customer (Northstar Financial, Dedicated) is requesting:

1) Ongoing SLO reporting for their Dedicated deployment (monthly preferred; weekly acceptable short-term).
2) Clear definitions of what counts toward availability/success rate, latency SLIs, and streaming reliability (incl. disconnects).
3) Whether Redwood tracks an error budget for Dedicated, and what happens operationally when the budget is burning/exhausted.
4) How routing/fallback, retries, and client-side aborts are treated in SLO accounting.

Customer context:
- Enterprise procurement + risk team is asking for “evidence” that we measure SLOs and have a defined policy for breaches (error budget + escalation).
- They want per-region visibility and a statement of exclusions (invalid requests, rate limits, customer aborts, etc.).

Requested deliverable:
- A written SLO/SLI definition doc suitable for vendor governance review,...

4) How routing/fallback, retries, and client-side aborts are treated in SLO accounting.

Customer context:

- They want per-region visibility and a statement of exclusions (invalid requests, rate limits, customer aborts, etc.).

Requested deliverable:
- A written SLO/SLI definition doc suitable for vendor governance review,...

comments.body: 14 PT — Rachel Kim (CS): Opened per Northstar’s request after QBR. They’re escalating internally because their risk team needs formal definitions + reporting. Markus can you take point with SRE/Obs? We should also track access needs (Console dashboard) separately.

02 PT — Markus Klein (Solutions Eng): Taking. I’ll draft customer-facing summary of SLIs/SLOs (non-contractual) and coordinate with Aisha/Allison on definitions + what we can safely share. Will also confirm whether we can provide a dedicated dashboard view or export while Console UX is in flight.

28 PT — Aisha Bello (SRE): We can share the SLI measurement spec and explain exclusions. Targets are still in final approval with Product (PM-812), but definitions are stable. For Dedicated, we can provide per-account/per-region SLO panels from the new tiered dashboards package; need RBAC enablement for their account + a read-only role.

44 PT — Allison Grant (Observability): Key clarifications to communicate:
- Success rate SLI is based on server-side final status: 2xx/3xx count as success; 5xx count as failure.
- 4xx generally excluded (invalid request / auth). 429 excluded from availability SLI but tracked separately as “limited requests”.
- Latency is measured at API gateway: request_received → response_complete for non-streaming; request_received → first_token and request_received → stream_complete for streaming.
- Client aborts are excluded when we can attribute disconnect to client-initiated close; server disconnects count.
- Retries: we account based on the first a...

10 PT — Markus Klein (Solutions Eng): Sent Northstar a 2-page write-up (customer-facing) covering SLI definitions, exclusions, and how streaming disconnects are classified. Also included a note that SLO targets are being formalized and will be reflected in their contract/SLA renewal language; current numbers are operational goals and may adjust.

Asked them to confirm desired cadence (monthly PDF export vs read-only dashboard access).

22 PT — Rachel Kim (CS): Northstar prefers dashboard access (auditability + self-serve), plus a monthly export for governance. They asked specifically for per-region p95 and a statement that 429s are excluded from availability but still surfaced.

Action: proceed with dashboard access request (tracked under INT-9331).

37 PT — Sergio Costa (Eng Platform): Dashboards support per-tier/per-region views, and we can filter to a dedicated account once RBAC is granted. We can also annotate deploys/incidents for audit trail. Export is manual today (Grafana CSV/PDF); Console-native export is not shipped yet.

05 PT — Markus Klein (Solutions Eng): Follow-up call with Northstar risk team. Walked through:
- Availability (success rate) definition and exclusions (4xx/429/client aborts).
- Latency measurement points for streaming vs non-streaming.
- How fallback is treated (success but tracked; customer can set policy to ‘no fallback’ if desired, with capacity implications).
- Error budget policy conceptually (burn-rate alerts, mitigation, and release gating) and what signals they can see.

They’re satisfied as long as they get consistent definitions + a repeatable report.

18 PT — Rachel Kim (CS): Confirmed access approval completed via INT-9331 and customer can see the Dedicated SLO dashboard panels for their account. Closing ticket; we’ll handle monthly exports via CS operations until Console export is available.

title: Live-traffic rehearsal and safe revert guidance for tenant adaptive optimizer flags
body:
Issue summary: Customer (VeloxAI, Dedicated) reports intermittent 95th percentile latency spikes and increased token cost after enabling an experimental adaptive optimizer bundle (dynamic batching + aggressive KV prefetch + mixed-precision quant profile). They want a safe plan to rehearse live-traffic changes and an atomic rollback procedure that minimizes tenant disruption.

Impact: Elevated tail latency (95p) from ~320ms to ~860ms for conversational endpoints, increased per-token cost by ~18% on observed traffic mix, some requests experienced streaming disconnects. No full 5xx incident but user-facing slowdowns and elevated error counts for long-session chat flows.

Environment: Dedicated pool, tenant pinned to model rw-inference/gpt-medium-8b-quant, autoscale enabled, routing policy: region-first with fallback to smaller-quantized variant. Change rolled out as feature-flagged optim...

Environment: Dedicated pool, tenant pinned to model rw-inference/gpt-medium-8b-quant, autoscale enabled, routing policy: region-first with fallback to smaller-quantized variant. Change rolled out as feature-flagged optim...

comments.author: Aisha Patel ()
comments.body: Acknowledged. Collecting traces and asking SRE to pull memory profiles. First response ETA within 4 hours.

comments.author: Diego Ramirez (SRE)
comments.body: Confirmed memory pressure on nodes 11-13. Found eviction thrash with kv_prefetch enabled. Proposing a staged rehearsal plan: 1) snapshot current flag state, 2) deploy conservative-batching to 5% traffic, 3) monitor 95p for 30m, 4) if safe, increment to 20% and run for 1h.

comments.author: VeloxAI (customer)
comments.body: Can we rehearse with replay tooling instead of real traffic? We prefer no user-visible slowdowns for now.

comments.author: Diego Ramirez (SRE)
comments.body: Rehearsal options: a) Traffic replay on a shadow cluster (recommended) with same distribution; b) Live 5% canary. Shadow replay requires a staging cluster sized to match peak working set—we provided sizing guidance. Both approaches will include an automated abort if 95p or error rate crosses thresholds.

comments.author: Aisha Patel ()
comments.body: Customer opted for shadow replay. Provided replay checklist and script. Next steps: schedule replay window and SRE to observe metrics.

title: Step-back procedure request: mixed-precision optimizer + KV prefetch causing cost/latency variance for BrightLedger
body:
Issue summary: BrightLedger reports a sudden increase in per-token billing and intermittent P95 latency spikes after enabling a mixed-precision optimizer profile (adaptive-mix) combined with tenant-scoped KV prefetch. Impact: higher cost, increased tail latency for interactive routes (chat endpoints), and customer urgency to revert with minimal disruption.

Observed at ~2026-03-09 22:00 UTC when tenant opt experiment was promoted to 100% for BrightLedger. Customer requests a safe, staged step-back plan and assistance to execute it on Dedicated capacity without broad service disruption.

comments.body: 12 Nora Chen: Created ticket after BrightLedger raised via enterprise-support channel. Provided initial telemetry and billing delta.
47 Ravi Menon: Assigned. Will validate logs and run staging replay first. Tentatively plan a soft hold approach to avoid full rollback.
05 SRE (Liam O'Connor): Observed same eviction pattern on staging replay. Recommend disabling kv_prefetch as first action.
30 BrightLedger (Customer): We prefer a plan that avoids session re-creation if possible; please advise expected UX changes during step-back.
20 Ravi Menon: Responded with plan: soft hold + batch reduction + optional traffic split to minimize session churn. Provided timeline and rollback commands.
00 Nora Chen: After Phase 1 the P95 improved from 430ms -> 160ms for critical route; billing estimates trending down. Preparing snapshot restore as next-step if metrics stabilize further.

title: Enterprise customer unable to update routing policies (control plane config write failures, 500s)
body:
Issue summary
- Zenlytics (enterprise) cannot create/update routing policies in the Redwood control plane. UI and API requests fail with 500 errors.

Customer impact
- Customer is blocked from: (a) updating routing policy weights, (b) pinning model version for a new rollout, (c) adding a new fallback region.
- Live inference traffic continues to serve, but customer cannot make policy changes and is stuck on last-known-good policy config.
- Customer indicates an urgent business need to shift traffic due to performance issues on a model variant; inability to change policy is time-sensitive.

Timeline (UTC)
- 2026-01-29 16:05: Customer reports in shared Slack + opens support ticket.
- 16:12: Support reproduces via API; escalated to SRE + Platform Control Plane.
- 16:25: Incident channel created; initial mitigation/workaround attempted.
- 17:10: Root cause narrowed to control plane config...

- 17:10: Root cause narrowed to control plane config...

comments.body: 12 UTC — Dev Patel (Support): Acknowledged. We can reproduce 500s on routing policy update in prod us-east. Pulling in SRE + Platform Control Plane. Request IDs: 01JH2K6Q7S2W9N7D2K6D9M0FQK, 01JH2K7A2F0S8Y3K1C1C5WQ8TX.

20 UTC — Rafael Mendes (SRE): Treating as Sev1 (customer blocked from config changes). Creating internal incident INC-2026-01-29-CP-CONFIG-WRITES. Please keep request-id in all updates; we’ll correlate in logs.

33 UTC — Ethan Park (Platform Control Plane): Confirmed failing in policy persistence step after authz passes. Seeing transaction timeouts correlated with large payload sizes. Investigating audit-log write path; will propose hotfix.

42 UTC — Rachel Kim (CS): Customer tried splitting updates (PATCH smaller changes) but still sees intermittent failures. They need to shift traffic today due to latency issues on current model variant.

12 UTC — Ethan Park (Platform Control Plane): Root cause likely audit log payload size + strict transaction timeout. Implementing hotfix to summarize audit diffs and async full payload storage; aiming to deploy to us-east first.

07 UTC — Rafael Mendes (SRE): Hotfix deployed to control plane API (us-east). Monitoring 5xx rate on write endpoints; initial drop observed. Please ask customer to retry policy update and report request-id if any failures persist.

18 UTC — Dev Patel (Support): Customer confirms API policy update now succeeds. Console still failed once; likely needs UI retry/backoff improvements. Logging follow-up with Console team separately if it persists.
12 UTC — Ethan Park (Platform Control Plane): Console retry/backoff patch rolled out. Also added request-id propagation to UI error toast so Support can triage faster next time.
05 UTC — Dev Patel (Support): 24h check: no further write failures reported; control plane write 5xx back to baseline. Proposing we resolve and keep monitoring for 48h.
40 UTC — Rachel Kim (CS): Customer confirms stability and successful policy changes through weekend. OK to close on our side.

10 UTC — Rafael Mendes (SRE): For taxonomy/ownership mapping: logging this as Control plane outage > Config write/CRUD failures (primary owner Platform Control Plane). This incident will be referenced in the owner mapping table to avoid misclassifying as Routing fallback misbehavior.

title: Intermittent dedicated-pool throughput collapse during short burst traffic due to long-lived ephemeral batchers
body:
Issue summary:
Customer BrightNote reports repeated p99/p50 throughput collapses in their reserved dedicated pool that correlate with short bursty traffic patterns from their webhooks. Impact: production API responses slowed and several timeouts observed in their app (fail-open fallback used).

Impact:
- Customer-facing model endpoints saw a 60-90% drop in tokens/sec for 3-7 minute windows during small spiky bursts (100-400 small requests/sec).
- Several requests timed out at client after 8s (customer set strict client timeout).
- No large-scale GPU failures or OOMs reported.

Environment:
- Dedicated pool: reserve-3 (8x A100s, mixed-tenancy), region us-east-1, pool autoprovision enabled.
- Customer traffic pattern: many short streams (~8-40 tokens) with high concurrency; they use streaming responses and model pin redwood-7b-v1.

Steps to reproduce (from customer):
1) Send 300 concurr...
